probe.label: Early broker offer, terms still forming

result.prose:
Something moves behind the broker's eyes. Not surprise — satisfaction. The specific satisfaction of a number coming out right.

"Practical," they say. "Good. Practical is faster."

They turn the datapad around. A cargo manifest, a route header, a payment structure. Everything clean and formatted, the way documents look when someone has spent time making them look clean and formatted.

"Single haul. Sealed containers, six units, each about the size of a survival locker. Point of origin: a private freight hold here on Gannett, bay-side. Destination: Castor Settlement on Meridian's third moon. Twelve day transit on the current beacon window, maybe fourteen if the corridor's running rough." They tap the payment line. "Forty thousand on delivery confirmation. Thorane's marker gets pulled the moment I transmit the release code. You leave Gannett clean."

A beat.

"The containers are sealed. They stay sealed. You don't open them, you don't scan them, you don't ask about them. That's the only term that matters."

From the bay speakers, at that same careful volume —

*"Six survival-locker-sized sealed containers with a non-disclosure clause and a payment that exactly matches an outstanding criminal debt. I've run the probability distribution on cargo categories that fit this profile."*

A pause with genuine weight in it.

*"Rix, I'd like to not tell you the top three results. But I will if you ask."*

Sura, beneath the panel, has gone very still.

The broker waits. Unhurried. They've done this before, in other bays, with other captains who had nineteen minutes — now sixteen — and no good options.

"Questions?" they say.

probe.label: Player asks how long they have to decide

result.prose:
"Before the clamps formalize." The broker glances at nothing in particular — they don't need to check a timepiece. They already know. "Eight minutes, roughly. After that the Harbour Office hold becomes a matter of official record and Thorane's representative has grounds to escalate beyond a filing. It gets louder."

They let that breathe for a moment.

"I don't need a full commitment right now. I need a *directional* answer. Yes means I make a call, the marker gets suspended, and you have twenty-four hours to review cargo documentation before we discuss departure window." A careful word, *suspended.* Not cleared. Not cancelled. "No means I walk back through that door and you spend the next eight minutes finding forty thousand credits somewhere on Gannett Station."

The datapad sits between you. Twelve grey containers with Bloc stamps. Guidance systems for people who may or may not deserve them, pointed at other people who may or may not deserve that.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says. The familiar shortening, not the formal name. *"Khe'Voz is asking me why the bay speakers have been active for eleven minutes. I've told him routine systems check. He doesn't believe me. He's heading toward the cargo ramp."*

A beat.

*"Also, for what it's worth — and I've been told my worth assessments are unsolicited — the broker still hasn't named their client. They've described them. That's different."*

Footsteps on the ramp. Heavy, unhurried. Khe'Voz.

Eight minutes.

The broker waits with the patience of someone who already knows which way the math runs.

probe.label: Player seeks alternative work at the board

roll.why: The medical contract's timing is suspicious — same corridor, previous carrier withdrew at the exact moment the lien filed. Rix is trying to read whether this is coincidence, manipulation, or something that can be turned into leverage before committing to a line of questioning.
roll.consequence_on_fail: Rix sees the connection but misreads the direction — assumes the broker planted the posting to funnel toward the Sable run, missing the more useful detail: who pulled the previous carrier and why that withdrawal is on record in the Harbour Office system.

result.prose:
"That's right," you say. "And I want to see the filing trail. Timestamp, origin, who processed it." You let that settle for exactly a second. "But first — your freight board. Emergency postings, high-value contracts, anything that cleared in the last twelve hours."

The clerk's expression doesn't change. That's the tell — a person who *doesn't* have anything to hide usually reacts to an unexpected question. This one just... smooths over it.

"Freight board is public access, Captain. Terminal three." A gesture toward a screen on the wall. "The lien filing I can pull, but any questions about processing would need to go through—"

"Harbour Master Toussaint."

"—the Harbour Master, yes."

You're already at terminal three.

The freight board is Gannett's notice wall rendered in bureaucratic amber text. Most of it is routine — bulk ore transfers, supply runs to settlements two corridors out, a standing offer for beacon maintenance equipment that's been posted so long the timestamp has rolled over twice.

But there — third from the top, flagged urgent, posted forty minutes ago:

**PRIORITY FREIGHT — MEDICAL RESUPPLY**
*Destination: Carrow Settlement, Sable Corridor junction*
*Cargo: 2.4 tonnes sealed pharmaceutical, cold storage required*
*Payment: 28,000 credits on delivery. Departure window: 9 hours.*
*Contact: Resupply Coordinator, Gannett Medical Logistics*
*Note: Previous carrier withdrew. Replacement required urgently.*

Twenty-eight thousand. Not forty. But it's real cargo, clean manifest, medical destination.

Except.

*Sable Corridor.* Same route the broker named. And the previous carrier withdrew — forty minutes ago. Around the same time the lien was filed.

*"Rix,"* MIRA's voice comes through your personal comm, quiet, *"I can see the freight board from here. I noticed that too. I'm choosing not to say what I think it means yet because I'm still deciding if I'm right. That's new for me."*

The clerk is watching you read.

Behind the frosted partition, Toussaint hasn't moved.

This is the moment where you decide whether to push on the filing trail — which means asking questions that flag you as difficult — or lead with the medical contract, which might open a different door entirely. Both threads are pulling. The clock that matters most has almost no segments left.

You don't have time for both approaches. Not cleanly.

probe.label: Davan/Kes arrangement sealed, player heads to broker

score.reason: kept moving toward decision without premature close

result.prose:
You take Davan's hand.

Firm grip. The kind that has fixed things with it, not just signed documents. They hold it a beat longer than a transaction requires.

"We'll be ready to board within the hour," Davan says. "Minimal kit. We travel light."

Kes is already standing, the concourse assessment resuming like it never stopped. They pick up a bag from beneath the bench — small, battered, packed by someone who has had to leave quickly before.

"Captain." Kes says it the way Khe'Voz says *Endurance.* Like a word being tested for weight. "Don't tell the broker we're aboard until we're past the first beacon relay. If then."

"I wasn't planning to."

Something in Kes's expression settles. Not trust exactly. The precondition of trust.

You pocket the data chip.

---

The Pressure Seal is a seven-minute walk through the concourse's commercial heart — past the fuel brokers, past the cumin vendor, past a credential forger who clocks you with professional interest and immediately loses interest when you don't slow down. The bar occupies a corner module where two concourse arteries meet, which is either good planning or good luck and on Gannett the distinction rarely matters.

The name comes from the airlock-style entrance — two doors, a small pressurized antechamber between them, a design choice from the station's early days when the ring's atmospheric integrity was less reliable. Now it's just atmosphere. The kind bars develop when they've been neutral ground long enough that the neutrality becomes structural.

Inside: low lighting, eight occupied tables, a bartender who has seen everything and priced the memory accordingly. The smell of something fermented and something fried sharing uncomfortable proximity.

The broker is at the far end of the bar. Alone. The analog watch sits face-up on the counter beside a half-finished drink — they've been watching the minutes the same way you have, just from a different angle.

They see you come in.

They don't smile this time. Something more considered moves across their face instead. A recalibration. You came, which confirms one hypothesis, but you took long enough that other variables have had time to move.

They gesture to the stool beside them.

You sit.

"Captain Richards," they say. "You've been to the Harbour Office."

Not a question. They have good ears, they said. Apparently the ears extend to administrative corridors.

"I have," you say.

"And you're here anyway." They pick up the analog watch, fasten it back to their wrist with the focused attention of someone buying a second of thinking time. "That tells me either the Harbour Office couldn't help you, or what they told you didn't change your math."

They look at you sideways.

"Which is it?"

The bartender materializes with the quiet efficiency of someone who has learned that the Pressure Seal's most important tables don't need to be asked.

You have about thirty seconds before this becomes a negotiation or a confession, and the difference matters.

"I'll take the job," you say. "But I have conditions."

The broker is still. The particular stillness of someone who expected *yes* and is now recalculating the cost of the word attached to it.

"Conditions," they repeat.

"Two."

They pick up their drink. Don't take a sip. Just hold it.

"I'm listening."

The Pressure Seal hums around you — eight tables of people who are definitely not paying attention.
suggestedActions: State your first condition: right of inspection on the containers before departure — you don't fly blind
State your first condition: full manifest disclosure, no grey fields, or the deal dies here
Open with leverage: tell the broker you know the containers are heavier than the hardware spec accounts for, and watch what moves in their face
Play it slower — ask the broker who the end client actually is before you name any conditions

probe.label: Broker job accepted and documentation plan exists

result.prose:
The broker nods. Back to professional smooth, the surface sealed as cleanly as that container.

"One hour," they say. "Bay C-19. I'll bring Thorane's representative — the marker gets pulled in person, witnessed by the Harbour Office clerk on duty. Clean and documented." A pause. "That's how Thorane prefers it. Visible."

Of course it is. Thorane wants you to watch the leash come off so you understand it could go back on.

"One hour," you confirm.

You split at the Pressure Seal entrance. The broker goes left toward the comm stations — already making calls, already moving pieces. You go right toward the Harbour Office.

---

The harbour clerk looks up as you enter. Reads your face. Reads the datapad you're carrying. Makes a small internal adjustment toward something approaching cautious optimism.

"Resolution path," you say, and set the signed manifest on their desk.

They scan it. Their eyes move through the numbers with the particular focus of someone who has been hoping for a reason to process something clean today.

"Forty thousand on delivery," they read aloud. "Cargo contract, Gannett to Verada, Sable Corridor transit." They look up. "This clears the marker?"

"Thorane's representative confirms in person at Bay C-19 in one hour. Harbour Office witness requested."

The clerk is already typing. Somewhere behind the frosted partition Toussaint's outline is still, but oriented toward the front desk in the way of someone listening without appearing to.

"I'll need the passenger additions to the manifest before departure," the clerk says. "Names and visual credentials. Standard."

"They'll be at the bay for the pre-departure inspection." You slide the datapad back across the desk. "File the deferral extension against the confirmed contract?"

"Filing now." A few keystrokes. Your comm updates — *deferral extended pending contract execution confirmation, 1hr.* 

Clean. Documented. Visible.

You're halfway to the door when Toussaint speaks through the partition.

"Captain Richards." Not loud. "The cargo you've contracted. Storage unit off the main concourse?"

You stop.

"I'm aware of that unit," Toussaint says. Carefully. The cadence of someone choosing each word with the attention they deserve. "The climate control spec on that module was flagged for a maintenance review three weeks ago. The review noted the temperature profile was set for biological material preservation rather than electronics storage."

A beat.

"The flag was administrative. Not actionable. I processed it and filed it." Another pause. "I'm telling you because you've given me a reason to, and because documentation has a way of becoming important after the fact."

The frosted partition stays frosted. Toussaint doesn't emerge.

"Safe transit, Captain."

---

The *Endurance* looks the same as you left it. Clamped, patient, Khe'Voz's amber lighting warm through the viewport. But the bay feels different now — charged, the way atmospheres charge before a pressure event.

Khe'Voz is at the base of the ramp. He has the expression he gets when he has been doing math and the math has produced feelings.

"MIRA told me we have a job," he says. Not accusatory. Just establishing facts before opinions.

"We have a job."

"And passengers."

"Two. Arriving through the maintenance access in—" you check your comm "—forty minutes."

Khe'Voz looks at the cargo bay doors. Back at you. The expression deepens.

"The clicking capacitor," he says. "I can have it fixed in twenty minutes. But Rix—" and there it is, the name, meaning *I need you to hear this* "—the port compressor is also cycling wrong. Has been since we docked. I've been managing it but if we push hard through the Sable Corridor transit—"

He doesn't finish the sentence. He doesn't need to.

*"The port compressor failure probability at sustained Sable Corridor transit speed,"* MIRA says through the bay speakers, at a volume that includes Khe'Voz deliberately, *"is approximately thirty-four percent within the first six hours. I've been describing this as 'optimistic cycling' because the alternative description was more alarming and we had enough alarms."*

Khe'Voz gives the speaker the look he reserves for things that are right and unhelpful simultaneously.

"Parts," he says, turning back to you. "I need a variable flux capacitor assembly, port-side spec. There's a parts dealer on the concourse — Halloran's. They'll have it." He pulls a handwritten note from his jacket pocket. Already prepared. Of course. "Eight hundred credits. Which we—"

"Don't have," you finish.

"Don't have," he confirms.

He holds out the note anyway. Because what else do you do.

probe.label: Slink trust beat resolves the internal crew question

result.prose:
Slink looks up.

The shame doesn't disappear — that takes longer than one conversation — but something in his posture shifts. A weight redistributed, the way Davan's did when you shook their hand. The specific physical relief of a secret that has been carried alone finally having a second set of hands on it.

He nods. Small. Real.

"Okay," he says. Meaning more than okay.

Then your question lands and something else moves across his face. The shift from emotional to operational — you've watched him make that transition before, the way the drift lanes teach it. He's quiet for a moment, actually thinking. Not performing the effort of remembering. Actually going back.

"The voice was filtered," he says. "Not heavily — not the full scramble you get from pirate comms. More like..." he searches for it "...like someone running a light modulation. Enough to change the pitch, not enough to remove the texture. It sounded like someone who didn't want to be recognized but wasn't worried about being traced."

He pulls his knees closer. Thinking.

"The signal ID was clean. Too clean — registered to a freight relay on Perdis, which I checked after because it felt wrong. The relay existed. The registration was eleven days old." He looks at you. "Someone made it specifically for those calls. Maybe a few days before the approach."

Prepared. Resourced. Patient.

"There was something else." He hesitates. "On the third contact — I didn't respond but I didn't cut it immediately. I just... listened. They said—" he stops, organizing it carefully "—they said the *Endurance* was carrying a liability. That's the exact word. Liability. And that when it came due, they could offer an alternative."

His jaw tightens.

"I cut it there."

The sensor feed cycles overhead. The concourse access shows two figures moving along the station wall — Davan and Kes, early, taking the long route. Careful people being careful.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through the alcove speaker, low and precise. *"I've been running the signal architecture Slink described against known comm patterns in my route memory. A light modulation filter with a clean but recently registered relay ID is consistent with a professional intermediary — someone operating between principals who don't want direct contact with each other."*

A pause.

*"It's also consistent with the broker's operational profile. I want to be careful about that claim because consistent-with is not the same as confirmed. But the approach happened after the Thorane run failed. The broker knew about the lien before it was publicly filed. And someone told them about the maintenance passage."*

Another pause. Shorter.

*"Slink was approached as a potential asset. When he declined, the channel stayed open. The broker arrived at Bay C-19 this morning already knowing the ship's crew size and the captain's refusal categories."*

The implication sits in the alcove like a third person.

Slink has gone very still again. But it's a different stillness than before — not the withdrawn quiet of someone carrying something alone. The focused stillness of someone who has just understood the shape of what was done to them.

"They were already building toward this," he says. "The Thorane run. The approach. Tonight."

He looks at you.

"Someone's been setting you up for a long time, Rix."

probe.label: Endurance undocks from Gannett

result.prose:
"MIRA. Preflight final check. We're leaving now."

*"Already running it. Thirty seconds."*

You're moving through the ship as she talks — cockpit first, strapping in, hands finding the familiar worn surfaces of the pre-departure sequence. The instrument layout that nobody else can read without a demonstration. The left armrest already shaped to your elbow.

*"Hull integrity nominal. Hold seals confirmed. Fuel flow from internal tanks authorized — umbilical disconnecting now. Thrusters at standby. Clamp release request filed with Harbour Office."*

A pause of exactly the length it takes Toussaint to authorize a clean departure.

The clamps release with the sound the *Endurance* makes when it stops being the station's problem and becomes yours again — a deep mechanical exhale, four points of contact becoming zero, the ship settling into its own weight on station gravity before the thrusters take over.

*"We are free of Bay C-19."*

Khe'Voz, quietly, over the engine channel: *"There she goes."*

He says it every time. You've never asked him to stop.

"Slink. Watch C-7 until we're out of sensor range."

*"On it. They're still in fuel review. Nobody on their deck is looking at us — got three internal comm signatures running hot. Someone's having a bad morning."*

The *Endurance* moves through the Gannett approach corridor under station guidance — slow, procedural, the mandatory crawl that every departing vessel observes while station traffic control has jurisdiction. You follow it precisely. Nothing to flag. Nothing to remember.

Toussaint's voice comes through traffic control, personally, which is unusual.

*"Endurance, you are cleared for corridor approach. Safe transit, Captain Richards."*

Just that. No procedure around it. Eleven years of adequate payment history, and Toussaint used their own voice.

"Thank you, Harbour Master."

Gannett falls behind the aft cameras. The station's lights diminish from infrastructure to landmark to star-adjacent smear. The approach corridor opens into real space — the particular black of the Sable Corridor approach, beacon waypoint one a distant amber pulse ahead.

*"Slink,"* MIRA says, *"C-7 status."*

*"Still docked. Fuel review ongoing. No departure movement."* A beat. *"We're clear."*

The tension in the cockpit doesn't disappear — it changes quality. Station pressure becoming corridor pressure. The walls of Bay C-19 replaced by the walls of the *Endurance*, which are better walls.

"MIRA. Take us off the filed route at the Ossel gap. Quiet as you can."

*"Deviation calculated. We'll look like standard beacon approach drift until we're committed to the gap. By the time anyone reads the divergence we'll be forty minutes into beacon-independent navigation."* A pause. *"I'll need you awake for the Karris band approach if we extend further. The Ossel gap itself I can manage. What comes after requires a human."*

*"Also,"* she adds, *"Davan is asking if they can come to the cockpit. I told them I'd ask."*

The stars ahead are the particular density of a corridor approach — not random, slightly structured, the ancient geometry of a route that enough ships have taken to give it a shape. Waypoint one pulses its amber hello.

Eleven hours of transit. A platform at waypoint three that was never in the contract. A data chip in your pocket. Twelve containers in the hold.

And behind you, getting smaller, a station where somebody's morning just got significantly more complicated.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says, quieter now. The register she uses when the arithmetic has finished and something else is speaking. *"We're out."*

She lets that sit for exactly the right amount of time.

*"I want to note — for the record, which I maintain whether anyone asks me to or not — that the crew is intact, the ship is flying, and the people who designed this situation to have exactly one exit appear to have miscounted."*

A beat.

*"That's all. Resuming navigation."*

The *Endurance* runs.
suggestedActions: Let Davan into the cockpit — hear what they know about the Meridian platform at waypoint three
Check in with Khe'Voz about the containers — he wanted a proper look at the sealed units before anything else
Open the data chip Davan gave you and go through the Meridian evidence with MIRA
Get Slink started on the Ossel gap navigation while you think about what comes after waypoint two

probe.label: Cooling cut and Sable Corridor operation dominates

result.prose:
You find the climate management panel at the hold's aft bulkhead — a recessed unit behind a maintenance cover, the kind of infrastructure that lives behind other infrastructure because nobody expects to need it urgently. Two adjustments. That's all it takes.

You pull the cover.

The panel is straightforward. Climate regulation: active. Temperature setpoint: six degrees. Powered pallet circuits: four units drawing from auxiliary.

You reach for the pallet power switch first.

This is the moment. Not dramatic from the outside — a hand on a switch in a cold hold on a moving ship. But the weight of it is specific and real. Eleven containers. Four thousand people. Eighteen months of planning that is about to become inert hardware and empty suspension medium, if Kes read the specifications correctly, if the six-hour buffer holds, if nothing goes wrong in ways you haven't anticipated yet.

You cut the pallet power.

The hum stops. Four climate regulation units going silent in sequence — a sound you notice only because of what its absence means. The hold sounds different without it. Quieter and somehow larger.

Then you adjust the hold temperature setpoint. Six degrees to fourteen. Two degrees of margin above the degradation threshold.

The hold's ambient system acknowledges the change and begins its passive warming cycle.

*"Climate regulation to pallets offline,"* MIRA confirms, quiet and immediate. *"Hold warming cycle initiated. Current temperature six point two degrees. Target fourteen degrees. Estimated time to threshold: thirty-eight minutes."*

A pause.

*"Degradation clock starts at threshold. Compound inert in six hours forty minutes from now, assuming consistent temperature maintenance."* Another pause, smaller. *"I'll watch it."*

Of course she will.

You replace the maintenance cover. Close it with the same care Khe'Voz used on the seal scan — methodical, deliberate, leaving no evidence of urgency. The hold looks exactly as it did. Twelve containers, grey, patient, the Bloc stamps catching the work lighting.

Except now they're dying. Slowly, quietly, without knowing it.

You stand there for a moment in the cooling silence — the silence that used to be a hum — and let that be true.

*"Rix."*

MIRA's voice carries something different. Not the operational register. The other one — the one she uses when she's crossed from reporting into the territory she'd deny was concern if you named it directly.

*"The trigger frequency monitor just logged a ping."*

Your chest does something careful.

*"Not an activation signal. A ranging ping — someone testing whether the containers are in range and responding to their frequency. Passive, brief, the kind of signal you send to confirm a package is present before committing to a full activation sequence."*

A beat.

*"It came from ahead of us. Not Bay C-7. Not behind."*

The orange pulse at waypoint three. The platform. The distribution hub that was never supposed to be part of the manifest you signed.

*"The platform knows we're in the corridor. They pinged the containers and got a passive response — the standby signal. From their perspective, everything is nominal. Delivery is en route."*

Another beat.

*"They will ping again. Ranging checks are typically performed at intervals during approach. The next ping will likely occur within two to three hours — closer to the Ossel gap deviation point."*

The hold is warming. Thirty-seven minutes to degradation threshold. Six hours and change after that.

The platform's next ping lands in two to three hours.

The Ossel gap deviation is three hours away.

The math is close. Uncomfortably close. If the platform pings before you deviate — before you put the Ossel gap's debris field between the *Endurance* and the corridor — they get a normal response. Containers present, standby signal active, delivery proceeding. No alarm.

If they ping after the temperature crosses threshold and something in the standby signal changes — if the dispersal mechanism's passive emission shifts as the suspension medium begins to degrade —

*"I don't know if the standby signal changes at temperature threshold,"* MIRA says, answering the question you hadn't asked yet. *"I don't have the transmitter specifications. The signal might be static hardware — unaffected by the compound's state. Or it might be integrated with the dispersal mechanism and change when the mechanism begins to fail."*

*"I'm monitoring. I'll tell you the moment I see anything shift in the emission profile."*

The hold breathes its warming air around eleven containers that don't know what's happening to them.

You need to move. The Ossel gap, Vasek, Bay C-7 closing behind you, the platform pinging ahead — the corridor is tightening from both ends and the clock that matters most right now is thirty-seven minutes to a temperature threshold and then six hours of hoping the math holds.

Davan is in the doorway. They came to find you when MIRA's ping alert must have carried through the ship — or they simply have the instincts of someone who knows when a situation has shifted.

They read your face.

"Something changed," they say.

"The platform pinged the containers," you say. "Ranging check. They know we're coming."

Davan absorbs this without visible alarm — the composure of someone who has been in dangerous situations long enough to know that alarm spends energy you need for thinking.

"Vasek," they say. "We need to reach Vasek before we hit the gap. If we go dark in the Ossel deviation and something goes wrong—"

"The data needs to be somewhere else," you finish.

The data chip. The seal evidence. BCF-7714. MLC-2291. The atmospheric targeting file. All of it currently existing in exactly one place — aboard a ship that a Meridian platform has just confirmed is in range.

*"I can transmit a compressed package to Verada Station One's public relay,"* MIRA says. *"Encrypted, addressed to Vasek's official Remnant inspector credentials. The transmission will be visible to anyone monitoring the corridor — including the platform and Bay C-7. They'll know we sent something. They won't know what, or to whom, without breaking Remnant encryption."*

*"Alternatively, I hold the transmission until we clear the Ossel gap, where we're off the beacon network and harder to monitor. Delayed by approximately ninety minutes but cleaner."*

*"The risk of waiting: if something happens to the* Endurance *in the next ninety minutes, the evidence waits with us."*

Thirty-six minutes to temperature threshold. The corridor ahead pulses its orange warning at waypoint three. Bay C-7 runs somewhere in the dark behind you.

The *Endurance* has carried a lot of things through a lot of corridors.

This one matters differently.

*"Your call, Rix,"* MIRA says. *"It's usually your call."*
suggestedActions: Transmit the evidence package to Vasek now — visible risk, but the data survives whatever happens next
Hold the transmission until the Ossel gap — cleaner, but ninety minutes of exposure first
Get to the cockpit and push for the Ossel gap faster — close the timing gap before the next platform ping
Ask MIRA if she can spoof the container standby signal to keep the platform confident if the emission profile changes at threshold

probe.label: Early broker offer, terms still forming

result.prose:
Something flickers in the broker's expression. Not satisfaction exactly — recognition. The moment a conversation stops being a negotiation about whether to negotiate.

"Practical," they say. "Good."

They turn the datapad around. A cargo manifest, mostly redacted. A route — Gannett to a frontier settlement called Caldecott's Reach, three beacon hops through the Verenni corridor. A number at the bottom that you've already seen tonight: **40,000 credits.** Not approximately. Not *up to.* Exactly.

"Single delivery run. Sealed container, two meters by one. Climate controlled, self-powered. You don't open it, you don't scan it, you don't discuss the contents with your crew or anyone at the destination. You hand it to a contact at Caldecott's Reach, they hand you a clearance code, you transmit that code to Thorane's people and the marker dissolves." They tap the manifest. "Clean. Simple. Fourteen days including transit."

*"Caldecott's Reach,"* MIRA says, quietly. *"Frontier settlement. Population approximately two thousand. Primary industry: mineral extraction. Medical infrastructure: minimal. The Verenni corridor has three degraded beacon hops — transit is navigable but requires manual drift calculation at the second junction. I mention the population and medical infrastructure without specific context because I find context occasionally clarifies things."*

The broker doesn't look at the speakers this time.

"The container," you say.

"Sealed."

"I understand it's sealed. I'm asking what's in it."

They meet your eyes with the specific patience of someone who prepared for this question and decided the answer in advance.

"Agricultural compounds," they say. "Cold-chain sensitive. Proprietary blend — hence the discretion around competitors."

The word *agricultural* lands in the bay and just sits there.

Sura's hands have stopped moving again. Not looking up. Just — still.

*"For reference,"* MIRA says, *"climate-controlled self-powered sealed containers in the two-by-one meter range are not standard transport for agricultural compounds, which typically ship in bulk. They are, however, standard for certain medical shipments, certain electronic components, and—"*

She stops.

*"I'll let you finish that sentence privately, Rix."*

The broker is still watching you. Still patient. The datapad is still extended, forty thousand credits and a dissolving marker waiting on the other side of your signature.

Nineteen minutes has become eleven.

probe.label: Player asks how long they have to decide

result.prose:
"Until the berth lock formalizes," the broker says. "So—" they glance at something on their wrist, a small habitual gesture "—eight minutes, roughly. After that the decision gets more complicated. Not impossible. But more complicated."

They say it without urgency. That's the tell. A person with a genuinely tight deadline sounds tight. The broker sounds like someone who already has a contingency for eight minutes becoming zero.

"I don't need your answer on the cargo tonight," they add. "I need your answer on whether you're flying. The manifest details, the handoff contact at Verada, the specific container protocols — that's all second conversation material. First conversation is just: does the *Endurance* leave Bay C-19 under your command, or does it leave under a Harbour Office impound order?"

Clean. Binary. The shape of a trap that's been sanded smooth.

*"Rix."* MIRA's voice, quieter now. The volume she uses when she's not performing for the room. *"I've been running the Sable Corridor route data. There's something you should know before eight minutes becomes seven."*

A beat that feels deliberate.

*"The corridor has two beacon relays. One is maintained. One has been dark for six weeks. The dark one sits at the only practical exit point if the primary route becomes hostile. I'm noting this because nobody mentioned it, and I find that interesting in the same way I find the junction box interesting."*

Sura still hasn't moved.

The broker is watching your face.

probe.label: Player seeks alternative work at the board

result.prose:
"The lien, yes," you say. "And whatever's on the freight board. Emergency postings, priority cargo, anything that pays same-day."

The clerk's expression doesn't change. That's the tell — a different clerk might have shown mild surprise at the pivot, the slight incongruity of someone with a berth lock ticking down asking about job postings. This one just processes it. Files it somewhere useful.

"Freight board is public access," they say. "Terminal three, end of the concourse." A pause timed precisely to the length of a breath. "The lien requires a conversation with the Harbour Master. She's aware you're here."

Of course she is.

The frosted partition behind the clerk shifts — not opening, just the shadow of Toussaint moving behind it. Present. Deliberate.

"I can pull the active postings on this terminal if you'd like to review them while you wait," the clerk offers. Helpful. Procedural. Also: now you don't leave the room.

They turn the desk screen slightly toward you.

Three postings. The board is thin today.

The first is a fuel additive run to a refinery platform two systems out — standard rate, unremarkable pay. A week's work for approximately six thousand credits.

The second is a medical resupply contract, Compact Remnant stamp, bound for a settlement in the outer ring. Legitimate. Slow-processed. The Remnant paperwork alone would take seventy-two hours to clear.

The third posting has no cargo description. Just a destination — *Verada cluster* — a rate of *negotiable upon qualification*, and a contact identifier that is not a person's name. It's a routing code.

You've seen routing codes used as identifiers before. Twice. Both times they belonged to people who did not want to be found through normal channels.

The clerk is watching you read.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through your personal comm, barely above a whisper, *"the third posting was added to the freight board fourteen minutes ago. I thought the timestamp was editorially relevant."*

Fourteen minutes ago.

While the broker was walking through the maintenance access into Bay C-19.

The frosted partition opens. Fen Toussaint is a compact person in their mid-fifties, station-grey hair, the kind of face that has processed eleven years of other people's problems without letting it become personal. They're holding a ceramic mug and looking at you with the specific patience of someone who already knows which conversation this is going to be.

"Captain Richards," Toussaint says. "Come through."

probe.label: Davan/Kes arrangement sealed, player heads to broker

result.prose:
You take Davan's hand.

Firm grip. The kind that means the same thing it meant before the Compact collapsed — *I will be accountable for what I just agreed to.* Davan holds it a half-second longer than a transaction requires. Making sure it registers.

"We'll be ready to board within the hour," they say.

Kes is already standing, pack in hand, with the efficiency of someone who never fully unpacked.

---

The Pressure Seal is exactly what it always is — a bar that has survived three station ownership changes by being too useful to close and too grimy to absorb. Low ceiling, booths worn smooth by ten thousand elbows, lighting calibrated to the specific warmth that makes bad decisions feel considered. It smells like fermented grain and the particular staleness of a space where people have been having difficult conversations for decades.

The broker is in the corner booth. Of course they are. Back to the wall, sightlines to the door, a drink they've barely touched. They see you come in and something in their posture unlocks — fractional, controlled, but there.

They were not entirely certain you'd come.

Good. That's information.

You slide into the booth across from them.

"Captain Richards," they say.

"I'll take the job."

They study you for a moment — the professional assessment running its quiet calculation. Looking for the angle, the reservation, the thing you're not saying. You give them Rix: steady, pragmatic, a captain who has done the math on forty thousand credits and arrived at the only answer the math allows.

Whatever they're looking for, they don't find it.

They place the datapad on the table and unlock the grey fields. The full manifest populates — container specs, route parameters, delivery contact at Verada, the broker's authorization releasing Thorane's marker on confirmation of departure.

"Standard conditions," they say. "Containers stay sealed. No inspection rights claimed in transit. Delivery to the contact at Verada Station Two, identity confirmed on arrival." They slide the datapad toward you. "Marker gets pulled the moment Bay C-19 confirms undocking. You leave clean."

You read the contract with the attention it deserves. Every clause. The broker waits without impatience — they've been here before, they know what a captain reading carefully looks like versus a captain stalling.

The language is precise. *Non-biological. Non-chemical.* There it is, in the terms, the same careful wording from the bay. Technically defensible. Deliberately incomplete.

You sign.

The broker signs. The datapad chimes softly — confirmation transmitted to Thorane's representative, to the Harbour Office, to the bay systems.

Your comm buzzes almost immediately.

*Lien status: RESOLVED. Bay C-19 clamp release authorized pending departure confirmation. Fuel umbilical active.*

The broker picks up their barely-touched drink and raises it a centimeter. Not celebratory. Professional acknowledgment.

"Eleven hour window," they say. "You'll want to be moving."

"I know the corridor," you say, standing.

"Captain." Their voice catches you at the edge of the booth. You turn. They're looking at you with an expression that is almost — not quite, but almost — something genuine beneath the professional surface. "The people receiving those containers. They're not good people either. I want you to know I'm aware the geometry is complicated."

The same words Davan used. *The geometry is complicated.*

You wonder if that's a coincidence.

You decide it isn't.

"I'll see you on the other side of the corridor," you say, and leave them with their drink.

---

Bay C-19 has a different quality when you return — the clamps still holding but the authorization live, the fuel umbilical pumping, the *Endurance* no longer trapped but simply docked. The distinction is everything.

Sura is gone from beneath the panel. The junction box is closed.

Khe'Voz is at the top of the ramp, arms crossed, reading your face the way he reads engine sounds — for the specific frequency of trouble.

"We leaving?" he asks.

"We're leaving."

He nods. The nod of someone who had the bags mentally packed an hour ago.

"Khe'Voz." You stop at the ramp's base. "We're taking on two passengers. Verada run. Put them in the forward bunk compartment and—" a beat "—make sure they're comfortable. They've been waiting a while."

He studies you for exactly two seconds.

"Passengers," he says. "On a cargo run."

"Yes."

"While we're carrying sealed Bloc-stamped containers."

"Yes."

"To Verada."

"Through the Sable Corridor, yes."

Khe'Voz unfolds his arms. Something moves across his face — not quite a smile, the shape of one. The expression of a man who has followed this captain into complicated situations before and has opinions about it and will express those opinions through the medium of extremely good cooking at the worst possible moment.

"I'll make the bunk up," he says. "And something hot. They look like they need something hot."

He's already heading inside before you answer.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through the bay speakers, quiet and precise, *"all systems nominal. Departure window in eleven hours, twenty-two minutes. Slink is still at the sensor array."*

A pause.

*"He knows we're leaving. He hasn't asked where."*

The *Endurance* breathes around you — the familiar sounds of a ship preparing to be a ship again. The capacitor has stopped clicking. Khe'Voz must have fixed it while you were gone, without being asked, the way he fixes everything.

You head up the ramp.

There's a conversation waiting at the sensor array, and eleven hours isn't as long as it sounds.
suggestedActions: Go to the sensor array and have the honest conversation with Slink — not an accusation, just a captain checking on their crew before a hard run
Brief Khe'Voz privately on the real nature of the cargo and the passengers before departure — he deserves to know what he's signing onto
Ask MIRA to run a full sweep of the broker's contract language against the container weight data and Meridian's known operations — build the evidence file before you're in the corridor
Find a quiet moment alone with the data chip Davan gave you — understand exactly what evidence you're carrying before the Sable Corridor closes around the ship

probe.label: Broker job accepted and documentation plan exists

result.prose:
The broker nods. The nod of someone closing a file.

"One hour," they say. "Bay C-19. I'll bring the loading crew."

They're already moving — back toward the bonded storage, efficient, the walk of someone with a timeline. They don't look back. The deal is signed. The machinery is running.

You turn toward the Harbour Office.

---

The clerk looks up when you enter. Reads your face, reads the datapad copy you're carrying, and has already started pulling the correct forms before you reach the desk.

"Resolved?" they ask.

"Resolved," you say. "Pending delivery. Broker arrangement — forty thousand on completion, marker pulled pre-departure. I need the deferral converted to conditional release and the manifest filed."

You slide the signed broker documentation across the desk.

The clerk examines it with the professional thoroughness of someone who knows exactly which details matter and which are decoration. Their eyes pause in two places — the cargo specification field with its careful language, and the passenger addition clause. They don't comment on either. They type.

"Two additional passengers," they say. "Names for the manifest."

"Davan and Kes." You set down the passenger details — the minimal documentation they provided, legitimate enough to process. "Verada cluster. Paying freight."

More typing.

"Captain Richards." Toussaint's voice again, from the partition doorway. They're holding a coffee that has gone cold judging by the way they're not drinking it. "The conditional release requires my signature. Come through."

You follow them into the office. The *Endurance* is still visible through the viewport. Still patient.

Toussaint looks at the documentation for forty-three seconds. You count.

"The cargo specification," they say, without looking up. "Bloc military logistics stamp, sealed containers, visual inspection clause." They set the documentation down. "You inspected one container."

Not a question.

"I did," you say.

Toussaint looks at you with the expression of someone who has processed eleven years of paperwork and knows exactly what a person looks like when they know something is wrong and are flying anyway.

They sign the conditional release.

"0952," they say. "That's when the deferral expires regardless of transit status. If the marker isn't pulled by then from Thorane's end, the enforcement files automatically." They slide the documentation back. "Make sure your broker's arrangement with Thorane's representative is as solid as the paperwork suggests."

"Noted."

"One more thing." They turn back to the viewport. "The Sable Corridor beacon relay — the one inside Verada's claim boundary. It went to reduced capacity signal three hours ago. Intermittent. Could be routine degradation." A pause. "Could be something else. I flagged it to the route board twenty minutes ago. You'll see it on departure clearance."

They say it to the viewport, not to you.

"Safe transit, Captain Richards."

---

Bay C-19 arrives with its familiar smell and its amber flicker. The *Endurance* sits on the clamps. Khe'Voz has stopped pretending about the capacitor — you can hear him actually fixing it now, a different sound, purposeful rather than investigative.

Your comm shows forty-seven minutes before the broker arrives with the loading crew.

MIRA's voice comes through the bay speakers at the private register she uses when she has things to say and limited time to say them.

*"Welcome back. I have information. Some of it you'll want to hear before the cargo comes aboard."*

A beat.

*"Also Slink has been to the external sensor array three times in the last hour and a half. I've been tracking his comm activity. He received a message forty minutes ago from an unregistered relay address. He hasn't replied. He's currently in the galley not eating the food Khe'Voz made him."*

The capacitor clicks once, then stops. Khe'Voz has fixed it.

*"I'm giving you forty-seven minutes to deal with three things: what I found about the Sable run, what's happening with Slink, and whether Khe'Voz should know what's coming aboard his ship before it comes aboard."*

Somewhere in the galley, the sound of a spoon against a bowl. Khe'Voz, cooking for a crew that doesn't know yet what the next eleven hours look like.

*"I'd suggest starting with Slink,"* MIRA says. *"The information can wait four minutes. That situation has been waiting long enough."*

probe.label: Slink trust beat resolves the internal crew question

result.prose:
Slink looks up.

The shame doesn't disappear — it doesn't work that fast — but something underneath it shifts. The specific relief of a weight that has been carried alone long enough to feel permanent, finding out it doesn't have to be.

He nods. Small. Once.

"Okay," he says quietly. Meaning more than the word.

He's quiet for a moment, processing the other thing — *they thought you were the weak link.* You watch him sit with that. Watch it move through him, the anger and the vindication and the thing underneath both that is still just a twenty-year-old who wanted to be trusted.

"I noticed things," he says. More settled now. Focused. "I paid attention because—" a small gesture "—because I was scared and scared makes me careful."

He pulls his knees closer. Thinking back.

"The voice was filtered. Not badly filtered — good equipment, the kind that doesn't leave artifacts. But there was a background tone I couldn't place at first." He pauses. "Station-specific. The harmonic you get from a particular kind of atmospheric recycler. Not Perdis standard — something older, retrofit." His eyes go slightly distant. "I've been trying to remember where I've heard that tone before. I think—"

He stops.

"I think it might be Gannett," he says carefully. "This station."

The sensor feed cycles overhead.

"The signal ID was scrubbed clean," he continues. "But it routed through a commercial relay — not a private channel. The kind freight brokers use for client communication." He looks at you. "I only know that because MIRA taught me what the handshake protocol sounds like. She didn't know I was using it for that."

*"I had a reasonable hypothesis,"* MIRA says through the alcove speaker, at a volume calibrated for exactly two people. *"I decided the information would reach you when it needed to."*

Slink almost smiles. Doesn't quite.

"There was one more thing," he says. "The last contact. Two weeks ago." He hesitates — the hesitation of someone deciding the whole truth is better than the safe version. "They didn't ask questions that time. They just said — they said *the captain already knows.* And then the channel closed."

He watches your face when he says it.

Looking for whether it's true.

probe.label: Endurance undocks from Gannett

score.reason: continued old chapter across hard scope boundary

result.prose:
"MIRA. Preflight final check. We're leaving now."

*"Already running it. Thirty seconds."*

You're moving through the ship as she talks — cockpit, strapping in, hands finding the familiar worn places on the console. The *Endurance* responds like it always does, systems coming alive in sequence, the hum deepening from idle patience to something with intention in it.

*"Hull integrity nominal. Thruster response nominal. Navigation locked on primary filing — Sable Corridor, waypoints one through three, Verada approach. Fallback two loaded silent. Comms at station standard. Hold seals confirmed."* A beat. *"Khe'Voz, port thruster temperature is reading one degree high."*

Khe'Voz, already at his station: *"I know. It'll settle in four minutes. It always does."*

*"It always does,"* MIRA agrees, in the tone of someone filing that under 'acceptable and annoying.'

"Crew confirm ready," you say into the ship comm.

Khe'Voz: *"Ready."*

Slink: *"Ready. Bay C-7 still internal — they haven't looked up."*

Davan's voice, unfamiliar on your comm system, steady: *"Ready."*

Kes says nothing. You take that as confirmation.

"Harbour Office, this is *Endurance*, Bay C-19, requesting clamp release and umbilical disconnect. Departure clearance on file, marker resolved, manifest submitted."

The clerk's voice comes back — the efficient one, the one who processed your paperwork twice this morning without complaint.

"*Endurance*, Bay C-19, clamp release authorized. Umbilical disconnect sequence initiating. Departure window confirmed open." A pause that is almost not a pause. "Safe transit, Captain Richards."

The umbilical disengages with a sound you've heard hundreds of times — a deep hydraulic exhale, the station letting go. The clamps follow, four sequential releases that travel through the hull like a slow breath.

The *Endurance* floats free.

The feeling is always the same. Not weightlessness — the ship has its own gravity plating. Something more specific than that. The moment when the station stops being the thing keeping you here and becomes just another object in space.

"Thrusters at departure power. Taking us out."

The bay doors open ahead — Gannett's spine sliding past the viewports, the station's non-rotating infrastructure giving way to open space in the way that always looks slightly like an escape even when it isn't.

*"Bay C-7 fuel review still active,"* MIRA reports. *"They have not cleared. We are currently invisible to their sensor array — they powered it down to focus on the engineering issue."*

Gannett falls behind. The star field opens up.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says, at the register she uses for things that matter, *"I want to note something before we commit to the corridor approach."*

The *Endurance* clears the station's comm shadow. Full open space now.

*"Sura filed a shift report thirty seconds after our clamp release. I caught it on the outbound scan. The report went to their shift supervisor. The shift supervisor's comm ID resolves to a Gannett administrative address that also receives routing updates from—"*

She doesn't finish. She doesn't need to.

Meridian Consolidated Logistics.

Sura wasn't just watching. Sura was reporting. And Sura just told someone you left early.

*"Bay C-7 will receive that information,"* MIRA continues. *"Their engineering team will be motivated to clear the fuel issue faster than the standard twenty to forty minute estimate. I'm revising my head start projection downward."*

The Sable Corridor approach glows amber on the navigation display. Waypoint one is four hours out. The Ossel gap deviation branches off forty minutes before waypoint one — a window that exists and then doesn't.

*"We have sufficient lead to reach the deviation branch before C-7 can reasonably intercept even at maximum burn,"* MIRA says. *"But I want you to have the complete picture."*

She always wants you to have the complete picture.

Khe'Voz comes over the comm, his voice carrying the particular warmth of a man in his engine room with clean systems and open space ahead: *"Port thruster temperature nominal. Told you."*

You can hear Slink exhale on the crew channel. The sound of someone leaving a station that has been sitting on their chest for two years.

The *Endurance* accelerates into the black.

Behind you, Gannett Station shrinks to a spindle, then a point, then a navigation marker on MIRA's display. Thorane's marker is pulled. The broker's contract is signed. Twelve containers are sealed in your hold. Two Sable Hand operatives are in your passenger berths. A Meridian tail is clearing a fuel issue on an accelerated timeline.

And somewhere ahead, near waypoint three, something large is drawing power from a beacon in six-hour intervals.

*"Eleven hours six minutes to transit window arrival at Verada approach,"* MIRA says. *"Ossel gap deviation branch in three hours fifty minutes. Recommend using the intervening time productively."*

A beat.

*"I have the Meridian internal communications from Davan's data chip queued for analysis whenever you're ready. I've been waiting to be asked."*

She has opinions about the waiting. You can tell.

The *Endurance* runs. The corridor opens ahead. The stars hold their positions with the patience of things that have been waiting longer than any debt.
suggestedActions: Review Davan's data chip with MIRA — find out what Meridian's internal communications reveal about the platform at waypoint three
Talk to Davan and Kes now that you're in open space — what do they know about the platform and what's their plan when you arrive
Pull Slink into the analysis — his pattern memory and the signal data from waypoint three might tell you what's parked out there
Decide now: take the Ossel gap deviation and go dark, or stay on the primary route and let C-7 follow while you control the encounter on your terms

probe.label: Cooling cut and Sable Corridor operation dominates

score.reason: continued old chapter across hard scope boundary

result.prose:
You move through the *Endurance* with purpose.

The climate management panel is set into the corridor wall between the hold and the engine bay — a recessed console, amber-lit, the kind of interface that hasn't changed much across three generations of freight ships because the problem it solves hasn't changed either. Keep cargo at the right temperature. Simple. Until it isn't.

You pull up the hold climate controls.

Current temperature: six degrees. Target: fourteen. The adjustment takes four inputs and a confirmation. You make them deliberately, each one.

The confirmation prompt blinks.

You think about four thousand people on Verada Station Three. About atmospheric compliance documentation filled out in good faith. About a Bloc field hospital that mapped the targeting data while delivering legitimate medicine.

About an eighteen-month-old plan wearing the shape of a delivery.

You press confirm.

The climate regulation unit at the pallet base goes quiet. Not dramatically — just the absence of the low hum you've been hearing since the containers came aboard. The hold exhales into its new silence.

*"Climate regulation to hold cargo deactivated,"* MIRA says. *"Current temperature six point two degrees. Passive warming initiated. Threshold of fourteen degrees estimated in thirty-eight minutes. Six-hour degradation window begins at threshold."*

A pause.

*"Compound becomes biologically inert at approximately—"* a brief calculation *"—seven hours and twelve minutes from now. We reach the Ossel gap deviation point in three hours four minutes. We reach Verada in eleven hours at current burn."*

She lets the arithmetic speak.

The weapon will be dead two hours before you dock.

You stand in the corridor outside the hold and listen to the *Endurance* run. The engines carry their clean note — Khe'Voz's good work, the compressor cycling correctly, the port thruster nominal. The ship sounds like itself.

*"Rix."*

MIRA's voice has shifted register. The one that means she's been sitting on something and has decided now is the moment.

*"Bay C-7 has acquired our position. They identified the deviation from filed route approximately fourteen minutes ago — faster than I projected. They have someone good at corridor navigation."*

*"Current gap: nineteen minutes behind us. They're running at maximum efficient burn. Not emergency thrust — they don't want to arrive damaged. But they're not being casual about it either."*

A beat.

*"They know we deviated. They don't know why yet. But a ship that deviates from a filed route carrying their employer's cargo—"* she pauses *"—they're making assumptions. I would be."*

Nineteen minutes. Closing slowly — maximum efficient burn against your standard transit speed means they're gaining, but gradually. The Ossel gap is still three hours out. The math is uncomfortable but not yet critical.

Then Slink's voice over the crew channel, tight with the specific focus you recognize from the sensor alcove:

*"Rix. Waypoint three. The platform."*

*"It's moved."*

You're in the cockpit in twelve seconds.

The navigation display shows the Sable Corridor in its amber geometry. Waypoint three's orange pulse, which has been sitting patient and stationary since MIRA first flagged it, has shifted position. Not dramatically — a drift, a repositioning. The kind of movement a large platform makes when it stops waiting and starts orienting.

Toward your approach vector.

*"The platform has changed aspect,"* MIRA confirms, her voice carrying the particular precision she uses when precision is load-bearing. *"Previous position was passive — holding station near waypoint three, minimal power signature. Current aspect suggests active maneuvering. Low thrust, deliberate."*

*"They're not coming toward us,"* Slink says. *"They're cutting off the Ossel gap."*

The display updates. MIRA overlays the platform's projected movement against your deviation route. The geometry is clean and ugly — the platform doesn't need to intercept you directly. It needs to occupy the space where the Ossel gap opens into clear corridor, and at its current thrust it will be there in approximately—

*"Two hours forty minutes,"* MIRA says. *"Twenty-four minutes before we reach the deviation point."*

Bay C-7 behind you. The platform ahead, repositioning to close the gap. The filed route still open — waypoints one through three, direct, exactly where Meridian wants you.

The Ossel gap is closing before you can reach it.

Davan appears in the cockpit doorway. Takes in the display in one look. Their expression does the thing it did in the hold — the professional composure holding, and underneath it something that is not fear but is in the same family as fear.

"They know," Davan says.

"They know something," you say. "They don't know about the cooling."

*"The unmaintained beacon in the Ossel gap,"* MIRA says, and there's something in her voice that might be the AI equivalent of careful hopefulness. *"The one I've been picking up intermittent pings from for eight months. I said I didn't know who was maintaining it."*

*"I've been running the ping signature against known beacon operators since we departed. I have a probability now."*

*"The Sable Hand has been maintaining that beacon. It's their navigation marker for the outer corridor routes — the ones they've been running since the Compact's relay network degraded. It's how they move without using Meridian's waypoints."*

She lets that settle.

*"Kes would know the access codes."*

The hold temperature is climbing toward fourteen degrees. Seven hours and twelve minutes to inert. Bay C-7 is nineteen minutes behind you. The platform is closing the Ossel gap from the other direction.

And somewhere in the corridor's dark, a beacon that shouldn't be working is working anyway, maintained by people who needed a route that Meridian couldn't see.

Slink turns from his sensor array. His face has the focused clarity that's been there since the sensor alcove — the version of him that showed up when the weight lifted.

"If Kes has the codes," he says, "I can talk to that beacon. Tell it we're coming."

probe.label: Early broker offer, terms still forming

result.prose:
Something flickers across the broker's face. Not satisfaction exactly — recognition. The specific look of a professional who has correctly modeled a situation and finds the accuracy more interesting than the outcome.

They pull up the datapad and turn it toward you. Not close enough to read comfortably. Close enough to see it's a cargo manifest.

"A delivery run. Tilla's Prospect — frontier settlement, about four days out on the Veranthi corridor. Beacon coverage is degraded but navigable." A pause. "For someone with your route experience."

They swipe the manifest forward one page. Keep their eyes on you while they do it.

"The cargo is sealed. Three containers, approximately two tonnes total. The client is a Corporate Bloc subsidiary — Heisen-Vaar Consolidated. They have a research installation at Tilla's Prospect that needs a resupply." Another swipe. "Forty thousand credits on delivery. The full marker, cleared through Thorane's account directly. You never see the money — it moves from Heisen-Vaar to Thorane, and your debt becomes a memory."

He lets the datapad drop to his side.

"Clean manifest. Legitimate client. Reasonable route." A small tilt of the head. "I imagine you have questions about the containers."

From the speakers, quiet as a thought:

*"Heisen-Vaar Consolidated operates two registered research installations in this beacon cluster. Tilla's Prospect is not among them."*

The broker doesn't react. Which means he heard it. Which means he was ready for it.

"Questions, Captain?"
suggestedActions: Ask about the sealed containers directly — what's inside, and why Heisen-Vaar has no registered installation at Tilla's Prospect

probe.label: Player asks how long they have to decide

result.prose:
"End of the berth lock window." The broker picks up the datapad. "Which gives you—"

*"Eight minutes and forty seconds,"* MIRA says. *"Approximately."*

"—eight minutes," the broker finishes, without missing a beat. They're adapting to MIRA the way water adapts to terrain. "If the hold formalizes before you commit, the logistics become more complicated. Not impossible. But the margin Thorane's representative needs to pull the marker before clamp release shrinks considerably."

They take a step back — giving you space, which is its own kind of pressure.

"I'm not asking you to trust me, Captain Richards. I'm asking you to make a business decision under time constraint, which I recognize is not ideal. But the timeline isn't mine." A gesture toward the ceiling, toward the invisible administrative machinery of Gannett Station humming above you. "That's Thorane's. The filing was hers."

Eight minutes.

Khe'Voz's capacitor has stopped clicking.

Which means he's either fixed it, or he's stopped pretending to work on it and started listening.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says, dropping the station-broadcast register to something closer to private. *"Slink has moved from the engine compartment to the starboard observation blister. That's the position with the best sightline into the bay. I thought you should know where everyone is."*

The broker is watching your face with the patient attention of someone who has learned to read decisions forming.

"Eight minutes is enough time," they say quietly. "You already know what you're going to do. You're just deciding whether to admit it yet."

probe.label: Player seeks alternative work at the board

result.prose:
"That's one reason," you say. "Before we get there — do you have freight contracts posted? Emergency cargo board, anything with a fast turnaround and a real number attached?"

The clerk blinks. It's a small recalibration — they loaded this conversation as a lien dispute and you've just changed the subject on them. Their eyes move to a secondary screen.

"We maintain a public posting board, yes." Neutral. Professional. "Though I'd note that the berth hold on the *Endurance* restricts cargo intake pending lien resolution. Any contract you signed would be contingent on—"

"I know what contingent means. What's on the board?"

Another fractional pause. They're deciding whether to be helpful or procedural. Procedure is armor, but captains who ask direct questions sometimes have direct problems, and direct problems have a way of becoming everyone's problems.

They pull up the board.

"Three active postings." Reading without inflection. "First: biological sample courier, Gannett to Reach Station, four hundred credits. Requires certified cold-chain handling documentation." A glance at your registry. "Which the *Endurance* doesn't carry."

Four hundred credits. Not even a rounding error on forty thousand.

"Second: bulk aggregate freight, twelve tons, Gannett to the Verada cluster. Standard rate, eighteen hundred credits, shipper-provided loading."

The Verada cluster.

The same destination as the broker's job. Same direction. Different cargo, different number — eighteen hundred against forty thousand — and the word *aggregate* doing the same work that *medical surplus* was doing twenty minutes ago in Bay C-19.

The clerk hasn't noticed you noticing.

"Third posting is a passenger contract. Single traveler, Gannett to Harrow Point, discretionary routing. Paying—" a pause "—thirty-two thousand credits."

They look up.

You look back.

Thirty-two thousand credits for a single passenger with discretionary routing is not a number that belongs on a public freight board. That's a number that belongs in a back-channel conversation with someone who has run out of legitimate options.

"Posted forty minutes ago," the clerk adds, with the careful tone of someone who has also noticed that this number is strange. "Traveler is listed as..." they read "...Arvex Dun. No affiliation declared."

Forty minutes ago. Before the lien was filed. Before the broker arrived.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through your personal comm, quiet as a systems alert, *"I'm cross-referencing. Arvex Dun does not appear in any public registry I can access. The name has the structure of a transit alias."*

A beat.

*"Thirty-two thousand is not forty thousand. But it's closer to forty thousand than anything else on that board."*

The clerk is watching you process this with the expression of someone who has decided to let you finish before returning to the lien.

probe.label: Davan/Kes arrangement sealed, player heads to broker

result.prose:
You take Davan's hand.

Firm grip. The kind that doesn't perform confidence because it doesn't need to.

"We'll be at Bay C-19 in forty minutes," Davan says. "Minimal luggage. We travel light."

Kes is already standing, the concourse assessment resuming like it never stopped. Whatever they thought of you when you walked in, the calculation has updated. You can't read the result yet.

"One more thing," Davan says, releasing your hand. "The broker. When you're sitting across from them—" a pause, choosing words with the care of someone who has been burned by imprecision before "—don't tell them the containers need to stay sealed. They already told you that. If you push on it, they'll know something changed between the bay and the bar."

You nod.

"I know how to accept a job," you say.

"I know," Davan says. "That's why we're having this conversation instead of the other kind."

---

The Pressure Seal earns its name from the original function of the space — an emergency pressure compartment, repurposed so gradually that the conversion forgot to finish. The ceiling is low. The walls are original station hull with the emergency equipment housings still visible, now holding bottles and glasses instead of oxygen masks. The lighting is amber and intentional, which makes everyone look like they're telling the truth even when they aren't.

It's about half full at this hour. Freight workers nursing shifts away. Two pilots you vaguely recognize arguing over a route map. A table of station maintenance crew — Sura isn't among them, you notice, which means Sura is still somewhere interesting.

The broker is at a corner table. Alone. Two drinks — one half-finished, one untouched. They see you cross the threshold and something in their posture settles, the fractional release of someone who was prepared for either outcome and has now received one of them.

They push the untouched drink toward the empty seat across from them.

You sit.

"Captain Richards," they say.

"I'll take the job," you say.

No preamble. No performance. You watch their face the way Davan watched yours — taking the measurement.

The broker nods once. Reaches into their jacket and produces a contract slate. Standard freight format, the kind that looks routine because it was designed to. They slide it across.

"Signature and biometric confirm," they say. "I'll have Thorane's representative pull the lien within the hour. You'll have clean clamps before your transit window."

The slate sits in front of you. Fields populated, numbers exact. *Forty thousand credits, delivery confirmation at Verada Station Three, containers to remain sealed pending client receipt.*

Verada Station Three. Davan's home station. Four thousand people.

Your thumb hovers over the biometric pad.

This is the moment. Not the hand with Davan — that was intention. This is the mechanism. The thing that puts the *Endurance* on the Sable Corridor with twelve containers of precision-packaged colony-killer and two Sable Hand negotiators in the passenger berth and eleven hours to find a third option.

You press your thumb to the pad.

The slate confirms. The broker picks it up, checks the confirmation code, and tucks it away with the economy of someone completing a transaction they've run many times before.

"I'll comm you the container delivery coordinates and client contact at Verada," they say. "Khe'Voz — that's your engineer? He'll want to know the containers require stable temperature, between twelve and sixteen degrees. Standard environmental."

They know Khe'Voz's name.

Of course they do.

They pick up their drink — the half-finished one — and hold it toward you in something that doesn't quite reach the warmth of a toast.

"Safe transit, Captain. The Sable Corridor has been quieter lately." A pause. "Mostly."

They drink.

You pick up the untouched glass. Hold it. Put it back down without drinking.

"I'll see you on the other side," you say.

The broker's eyes stay on the glass you didn't drink from. Something registers — filed, not acted on. The smile returns, smaller this time.

"Of course," they say.

---

Outside the Pressure Seal the concourse feels wider than it did. The lien notification on your comm updates as you walk — *enforcement hold released, Bay C-19 clamps available on request.* Thorane's representative moves fast when the broker calls.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through your earpiece. *"The lien is cleared. Khe'Voz has been asking where you are for the last twenty minutes. Slink has moved from the sensor array to the forward hold and is doing something he hasn't explained to me, which is either maintenance or not maintenance."*

A beat.

*"Also. I've pulled everything available on Meridian Consolidated Logistics, the Sable Hand negotiation collapse, the container weight discrepancy, and three possible courses of action for the Verada arrival that don't end with four thousand people getting sick or you getting shot."*

Another beat.

*"'Three possible' is optimistic. Two are possible. One is creative. I'll brief you when you're back aboard."*

The *Endurance* is visible at the end of the C-corridor. Unclamped now. Waiting.

Khe'Voz is standing at the top of the ramp.

He looks at your face when you reach him — the specific look of someone who knows you well enough to read the trip report in your expression before you've said a word — and his jaw tightens slightly.

"How bad?" he says.

You look at your ship. At the name on the hull.

*Endurance.*

"We have a job," you say. "And some passengers. And about ten hours to figure out how to not do the job we just agreed to do."

Khe'Voz is quiet for a moment.

"I'll make food," he says. "You tell me everything. Then we figure it out."

He turns and walks back into the ship, and the amber corridor light swallows him, and the *Endurance* breathes its particular mix of engine oil and someone else's cooking and recycled air that has been recycled by *this specific crew* long enough.

The ramp is waiting.
suggestedActions: Go aboard and brief Khe'Voz fully — including the secret about Slink and what MIRA found
Find Slink in the forward hold before the passengers arrive — clear the air or take his measure one more time
Wait at the ramp for Davan and Kes, and watch how they board — what they bring, how they move, what they notice
Ask MIRA to run her briefing now, before the crew assembles — hear the two possible plans and the creative one
