probe.label: Broker job accepted and documentation plan exists

result.prose:
The broker nods. The nod of someone who has gotten what they came for and is now in execution mode.

"One hour," they say. "Bay C-19. I'll bring the loading crew — four people, bonded, they won't speak to your crew beyond operational necessity." A pause. "The manifest filing with the Harbour Office is correct procedure. Do it."

They say that last part with the specific emphasis of someone who wants the paperwork clean for reasons of their own.

You part ways at the concourse junction. The broker moves toward the bonded storage unit with their comm already at their ear. You turn toward the Harbour Office.

---

The clerk looks up as you enter. Reads your face. Reads the datapad copy you've already forwarded to their system.

"Executing freight contract," they say. Fingers moving. "Forty thousand on delivery, marker contingent on—" a pause "—Thorane syndicate representative confirmation." They look up. "That confirmation has to come from their end before we release the clamps. I can't process the lien deferral as resolved until it does."

"How long does that take?"

"Depends on how fast the representative responds." A careful look. "They're on station. So. Fast, probably."

Behind the partition, Toussaint's outline is visible and still.

"Passenger additions to manifest," you say. "Two. Davan and—"

The clerk is already typing. Efficient. No questions about the names, which means either professional indifference or the names mean nothing to their system.

They should mean nothing. They're clean identities — you'd bet on it. Davan and Kes have been operating carefully enough that their Gannett footprint is a three-day-old transit lounge receipt and a freight board posting.

The clerk prints the updated manifest and slides it across.

"Harbour Office copy filed." They meet your eyes with the specific neutrality of someone who has noticed something and chosen procedure over curiosity. "Captain. The six hour deferral clock — it's been superseded by the contract execution. You're on standard departure clearance now."

A beat.

"Toussaint wanted me to tell you, if you came back with a deal—" they glance at the partition "—that the procedural review on the passenger posting you looked at earlier has been administratively closed. No further action."

The Verada passenger posting. Filed this morning at 0340. The shell registration Toussaint flagged.

Administratively closed. As of when, exactly?

You don't ask. You take the manifest and move.

---

Bay C-19 feels different when you return to it. The same metal box, the same scored deck plates, the same amber-white argument in the overhead lighting. But the clamps are going to release. The *Endurance* is going to fly.

Sura is gone from beneath the junction box. The panel is closed. The tools are absent. As if they were never there, except for a faint scratch on the panel edge that wasn't there this morning.

Khe'Voz is at the base of the ramp when you arrive, arms crossed, reading your face the way he reads engine sounds — looking for what's wrong underneath what's functioning.

"We're loading," you tell him. "Broker's cargo. Twelve containers, bonded loading crew, one hour."

He looks at the empty junction box panel. Then at you.

"Rix." He says your name the way he says the ship's name — like it means something specific. "The capacitor I've been pretending to fix. It's fine. Has been fine." A pause. "I was buying you time in case you needed the bay to look occupied."

Eleven years younger than you. Twice as smart about the things that matter.

"Two passengers joining us," you say. "Sable Corridor run to Verada. I'll explain underway."

He absorbs this. The cooking and engineering and good-spirit brain running its own calculations.

"And Slink?" he asks quietly.

The question lands with everything it contains.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through the bay speakers, calibrated for your ears only, *"Slink has left the external sensor array. He's currently in the forward hold. Which is where the broker's cargo will be loaded."*

A beat.

*"I thought you should know before the loading crew arrives."*

Khe'Voz is watching your face. He's heard MIRA. He hasn't moved.

One hour. Broker arriving with four bonded loaders. Davan and Kes coming through the maintenance access. Thorane's representative somewhere on station processing your contract confirmation. The *Endurance* about to become a closed environment carrying twelve containers that aren't what they're supposed to be, two passengers the broker can't know the real purpose of, a crew member who might be connected to all of it, and forty thousand credits worth of leverage pointed in every direction at once.

The transit window opens in eleven hours.

probe.label: Slink trust beat resolves the internal crew question

result.prose:
Slink looks up.

The shame doesn't disappear — that takes longer than one conversation — but something underneath it shifts. The specific relief of a weight that has been carried alone long enough to feel permanent, and then suddenly isn't.

He nods. Small. Real.

"Okay," he says. The word doing the same work Khe'Voz's *okay* did — more than it looks like.

He's quiet for a moment, and you can see him moving from the emotional register into the practical one. The mental shift of someone who has been sitting on information and is now being asked to actually use it.

"The signal ID was scrubbed," he says. "Properly scrubbed — not a cheap job. Whoever it was knew what they were doing with comms." He pauses, thinking back. "But the first contact — before they cleaned it up fully — there was a routing prefix. Just for a second. I almost didn't catch it."

He reaches into his jacket and pulls out a personal comm. Old model, scratched, the kind you buy secondhand because new means traceable. He's been keeping records. You notice that — he didn't trust his memory with this. He wrote it down.

He turns the screen toward you.

A partial routing prefix. Seven characters. Truncated before the full identifier resolves.

*"Rix."*

MIRA. Through the alcove's small speaker, barely above a whisper.

*"I recognize that prefix."*

A pause that is not processing time. MIRA doesn't need processing time for recognition. The pause is something else.

*"It's a Meridian Consolidated Logistics internal communications prefix. Specifically their field operations division."*

The alcove is absolutely still.

*"The same company whose stamp is on the containers in our hold. The same company that destroyed the Verada peace negotiation twenty-six months ago."* Another pause. *"Slink wasn't contacted by someone who knew about the failed run. He was contacted by someone who caused it."*

Slink is looking at the comm screen. Then at you. The pieces arriving in real time on his face — the shape of what was done to him, to the crew, to the run that became a forty-thousand-credit debt around your neck.

He wasn't the weak link they found.

He was the weak link they tried to *create.*

"They set up the run to fail," he says slowly. Not a question. Working it out loud. "And then they tried to get me to — what? Help them find out who else knew?"

"Find out who to blame," you say. "Or who to use next."

His jaw tightens. Twenty years old and learning what it looks like when something large and institutional decides to use a person as a tool without asking.

*"There's something else,"* MIRA says. Still quiet. The voice she uses when she's decided you need to know something and is also aware of what knowing it costs. *"The three subsequent contact attempts Slink mentioned. The timing of the last one — two weeks ago — correlates with the date Thorane's marker was formally assigned to a collections desk."*

A beat.

*"Someone at Meridian told Thorane where to file. They've been managing this from both ends. The debt isn't collection. It's positioning."*

Positioning for a job they needed a specific ship to take.

Khe'Voz has stopped whistling. The *Endurance* hums clean and steady around you both.

Slink looks at the partial prefix on his screen. Then up at you.

"What do you need me to do?" he asks.

probe.label: Endurance undocks from Gannett

result.prose:
"MIRA. Undock sequence. We're leaving now."

*"Undock sequence initiated. Clamp release in ninety seconds. Umbilical disconnect on my mark."*

You're already moving to the cockpit.

"Khe'Voz — engines to departure power."

*"Already there."* The *Endurance* shudders once, deep in its bones, as the drive systems come up from standby to live. The sound changes — the station hum underneath everything replaced by something internal, something that belongs to the ship. *"She sounds good, Rix. She sounds ready."*

"Slink — watch C-7's sensor array. The moment they come back online I want to know."

*"On it."*

You drop into the pilot's seat. The cockpit wraps around you. Hands find the controls in the dark the way hands do after enough years — not looking, just knowing.

The nav display shows the filed route glowing amber. Underneath it, silent, unregistered, the Ossel gap deviation sits in MIRA's memory like a held breath.

*"Harbour Office departure clearance confirmed,"* MIRA says. *"Fen Toussaint personally signed the release. There's a note attached to the clearance file."*

"Read it."

*"It says: 'File that contingency route, Captain.' That's the complete text."*

Of course it does.

*"Umbilical disconnect in five. Four. Three—"*

The fuel line releases with a hollow thunk that travels through the hull. The last physical connection between the *Endurance* and Gannett Station goes slack. For one suspended second you're neither here nor there — not docked, not flying, just balanced on the edge of both.

*"Clamp release."*

The clamps let go.

The *Endurance* floats free.

Bay C-19 falls away beneath you as the maneuvering thrusters fire in their careful sequence — Khe'Voz's sequence, the one he adjusted six months ago for the port thruster's particular personality. The bay doors open ahead, Gannett's spine sliding past on either side, and then the viewport fills with the specific black of space that exists between stations and corridors. Not empty. Never empty. Just waiting.

*"Clear of Gannett Station superstructure. Transition to main drive in forty seconds."*

On the sensor display, Bay C-7 sits dark and grounded. Fuel review still running. No sensor sweep. No departure activity.

Forty seconds never felt so long or so short.

"MIRA. The moment we hit the corridor threshold — Ossel gap deviation. Don't file it. Don't announce it. Just take it."

*"Understood. I'll make it look like a navigational correction for beacon variance at waypoint one. Routine. The kind of thing tired autopilots do."*

*"Main drive in three. Two—"*

The *Endurance* pushes. That's the only word for it — the main drive doesn't roar, doesn't thunder, it *pushes*, steady and committed, the way the ship has always moved when it means it. You feel it in the seat, in your spine, in the particular pressure against your chest that means you're going somewhere.

Gannett shrinks behind you.

*"C-7 sensor array just came back online,"* Slink says, his voice carrying the focused clarity you've come to recognize as his best self. *"They're— they're sweeping the bay. They're looking for us."*

A beat.

*"They found the empty clamps."*

You can almost hear it from here. The moment on that ship when someone looks at a screen and understands what the empty clamps mean and starts calculating how far ahead you already are.

*"Corridor threshold in four minutes,"* MIRA says. *"Ossel gap deviation ready to execute on your mark. Bay C-7 is— yes. They've emergency-cleared the fuel review. They're requesting expedited departure clearance from the Harbour Office."*

*"Toussaint is processing it,"* she adds, with something in her vocal register that might, in a human, be called dry. *"Standard processing time applies. Toussaint is very thorough."*

Khe'Voz laughs — short, genuine, the laugh of a man who has decided to enjoy the next eleven hours despite everything they contain.

Davan appears in the cockpit doorway. Takes in the viewport, Gannett already reduced to a lit spindle against the black. Their expression does something complicated and quiet.

"We're ahead of them," they say.

"For now," you say.

"For now is enough." They look at the nav display — the amber line of the filed route, the invisible deviation waiting underneath it. "The Ossel gap. You're taking it."

"Yes."

Davan nods. The weight redistribution again — not relief, recalibration. They're a person who has learned not to count on things until they happen and has developed genuine gratitude for the moments when they do.

*"Corridor threshold in ninety seconds,"* MIRA says. *"Waypoint one beacon nominal. Waypoint three still cycling — six hour interval, currently in strong phase. Something large at the platform is drawing power."*

*"And Rix—"*

Her voice shifts. The register that means she's been holding something and has decided now is the time.

*"I've been running the Ossel gap's unmaintained beacon against our previous transit logs. The intermittent pings I mentioned — I have eleven months of data on them. The signal has a pattern. It's not random equipment cycling."*

A pause.

*"Someone is using that beacon to communicate. Short burst transmissions, irregular intervals, always when the Sable Corridor's traffic is light. I haven't been able to decode the content — the encryption is old Compact standard, which I can work on during transit."*

*"What I can tell you is that the last transmission from that beacon was fourteen hours ago. Before the lien was filed. Before the broker arrived."*

The corridor threshold arrives.

*"Executing Ossel gap deviation now,"* MIRA says. *"We are off the filed route. Bay C-7 is following amber. We are on something else entirely."*

The *Endurance* turns — subtle, purposeful, the bearing change of a ship that knows where it's going even when nobody filed the paperwork.

The Sable Corridor opens ahead. Dark and full of things that haven't introduced themselves yet.

Behind you, Gannett is a distant light. Somewhere in Bay C-7, an engine is warming up eleven minutes too late.

*"Eleven hours, six minutes to Verada approach,"* MIRA says. *"I'll have the beacon encryption worked on by hour four. Khe'Voz — the port compressor is cycling at a frequency I'd describe as 'content.' I thought you'd want to know."*

*"I know,"* Khe'Voz says warmly. *"I fixed it right."*

The *Endurance* runs.
suggestedActions: Ask MIRA to prioritize cracking the Ossel beacon encryption — whoever was transmitting fourteen hours ago may know about the platform
Sit down with Davan and Kes and build a concrete plan for what happens at waypoint three
Check on Slink — give him something real to do and continue rebuilding what went quiet between you
Go to the hold and let Khe'Voz show you what he found when he did his own inspection of the containers

probe.label: Cooling cut and Sable Corridor operation dominates

result.prose:
You find the climate management panel at the hold's aft bulkhead — a recessed unit behind a service cover, the kind of infrastructure that exists to be forgotten until it matters. Three controls. Temperature setpoint, circulation mode, auxiliary power draw.

You look at it for a moment.

Not hesitation. Just the weight of the action being proportional to what it means. Somewhere behind you, eleven containers are listening for a frequency that will never arrive at a payload that is about to stop existing.

You reach in and adjust the setpoint.

Six degrees to fourteen.

The climate unit hums — then shifts register, the sound of a system stepping back from active cooling to passive ambient. The change is almost imperceptible. The hold will warm slowly, the way enclosed spaces do, indifferent to what that warmth is undoing.

*"Climate regulation cutoff confirmed,"* MIRA says. *"Current hold temperature: six point two degrees. Target threshold: fourteen degrees. Estimated time to threshold: forty-one minutes. Degradation clock starts at threshold."*

A beat.

*"Six hours and forty-one minutes from now, the payload is biologically inert. I'll mark the timestamp."*

She does. Somewhere in her memory, a clock starts running that has nothing to do with the Sable Corridor transit window or Bay C-7 or the platform at waypoint three.

It has to do with four thousand people on Verada Station Three who don't know this ship exists.

You put the service cover back. Careful. Exact. The hold looks exactly as it did before you touched anything. Twelve containers, grey, patient, the Bloc stamps catching the work lighting. The climate unit humming its new note at a frequency only MIRA is tracking.

You stand there for a moment in the cold that is already, imperceptibly, becoming less cold.

Then your comm activates.

Slink. Terse, focused, the voice from the sensor alcove that has fully replaced the withdrawn quiet of the last two months:

*"Rix. Bay C-7 has identified our deviation. They've left the filed route. They're running the Ossel gap approach vector."*

A beat.

*"And there's something else."*

His voice changes register — not alarm exactly, the specific flatness of someone reporting something they don't have a category for yet.

*"The platform at waypoint three. It's moved. It's not holding position anymore."*

*"It's heading toward us."*
suggestedActions: Push engines to maximum burn — widen the lead before C-7 and the platform can establish a pincer
Hail the platform on an open frequency — force them to respond officially before they can act
Go dark: cut all non-essential emissions and run silent through the Ossel gap to break their tracking
Contact Vasek at Verada Station One now — get the evidence moving before the corridor situation deteriorates further

probe.label: Early broker offer, terms still forming

result.prose:
Something in the broker's posture settles — not relaxation exactly, more like a mechanism engaging. You've said the thing that moves them from pitch to business, and they are, whatever else they are, a professional.

"Straight to it. Good." They pull up the datapad and turn it toward you, but not close enough to read detail. A tease, or a habit. "The job is transport. Single cargo unit, sealed, approximately two meters by one. Origin: a private storage facility on Gannett, sublevel four. Destination: Kerath Station, Spur Seven. You know the route?"

You know the route. Damaged beacon corridor, three drift jumps, five days minimum. Not popular. Not patrolled.

"The cargo requires discretion. No manifest entry, no scan logs, standard deniability protocol." They say *standard deniability protocol* the way people say *as per usual* — like it's boring, like you've both been here a hundred times. "Forty thousand on delivery. Clean transfer. The marker disappears. You leave Gannett with fuel in your tanks and nobody's name on your ship."

They lower the datapad.

"There's a timeline consideration. The cargo needs to move in the next —" they check something on their wrist "— four hours. Which aligns, I think you'll find, with your berth situation."

From the hull, that capacitor clicks.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says quietly. *"Kerath Station, Spur Seven. I should mention that's where the Verath Bloc maintains their primary colonial logistics depot. I'm not drawing conclusions. I'm providing geography."*

The broker waits. Patient as debt.

"Questions are fine," they say. "Within reason."

probe.label: Player asks how long they have to decide

result.prose:
"Before the clamps lock." The broker glances at nothing in particular — they don't need to check a timepiece. They already know. "Eight minutes, give or take. After that the Harbour Office formalizes the hold and my arrangement with Thorane's representative becomes considerably harder to activate."

They pick up the datapad. Don't offer it. Just hold it.

"I'm not asking you to decide about the geometry right now, Captain Richards. I'm asking you to decide about your ship."

It's a clean piece of leverage and they know it. Not cruel — *efficient.* The distinction is important to them, you can tell. They've told you more truth in the last five minutes than most brokers tell in a career, and now they're letting the clock do the rest of the work.

*"Rix."*

MIRA. Quieter than before. The register she uses when it's just you.

*"Khe'Voz is asking from engineering whether he should prep the drive for undocking. He says, and I'm quoting directly: 'tell the captain the capacitor is fine, it was never not fine, I was just giving her something to listen to.' He also says dinner is in forty minutes and he made the good protein."*

A beat.

*"He knows something is happening. He's asking in the only language he's decided is appropriate right now."*

Eight minutes. The broker. The clamps. Khe'Voz making the good protein and pretending the capacitor needed attention.

The broker waits. They're very good at waiting.

probe.label: Player seeks alternative work at the board

result.prose:
"That, and your job postings board."

The clerk blinks. A small recalibration — they were prepared for negotiation about the lien, not a pivot.

"The freight board is public access," they say, with the careful tone of someone who isn't sure whether to be helpful or suspicious. "Terminal's on the wall. But Captain, the berth hold—"

"Formalizes in—" you check "—about ninety seconds. I know. Humor me on the board first."

They don't stop you. Procedure doesn't require them to.

The freight posting terminal is mounted beside the door — a scratched screen cycling available contracts in Gannett's standard yellow-on-black. You scan it fast, the way you've learned to read these boards, filtering automatically for payout, destination, timeline.

Three postings. The first two are immediately wrong — a bulk ore transfer paying four thousand credits to a dead-end station, and a passenger run that wants a certified medical bay you don't have.

The third stops you.

**URGENT FREIGHT — PRIORITY POSTING**
*Origin: Gannett Station Bay A-07*
*Destination: Cassimer Relay, Outer Sable*
*Cargo: Sealed diplomatic correspondence, one (1) passenger, Compact Remnant authorization*
*Payout: 28,000 credits, partial upfront*
*Timeline: Departure within 14 hours. Hard deadline.*
*Contact: Remnant Administrative Office, Gannett*

Twenty-eight thousand. Not forty. But *Compact Remnant authorization* on a posting board means official clearance, which means the lien gets complicated in ways that might buy time or leverage.

It also means the Outer Sable. The same corridor the broker's job runs through.

The same corridor the Sable Hand operates.

You're still reading when the clerk says, quietly:

"Captain Richards. The hold just formalized."

And from behind the frosted partition, a chair moves. Unhurried. The outline of Fen Toussaint standing up.

The frosted door opens. Toussaint is exactly what eleven years in that chair looks like — not tired, not hard, just *settled* into the specific gravity of someone who has processed enough crises to stop being surprised by them. They look at you with the measured patience of a person who already knows the shape of this conversation.

"Rix." Not *Captain Richards*. Something in that. "Come through."

It's not quite an invitation. It's not quite an order. It's the kind of sentence that has a door built into it and expects you to walk through.

*"Rix,"* MIRA murmurs in your earpiece, barely a whisper, *"the Remnant posting and the broker's job share a destination corridor. I don't know if that's relevant. I've decided to mention things I don't know are relevant. It seems to be working out."*

probe.label: Davan/Kes arrangement sealed, player heads to broker

result.prose:
You take Davan's hand.

Firm grip. The kind that has done real work.

"Arrangement," you say.

Kes nods once from the wall — a soldier's acknowledgment, or close enough to it. They're already thinking about the next problem. You appreciate that about them.

"Bay C-19," you tell Davan. "Give me forty minutes. Come aboard through the main ramp, not the concourse access. Tell Khe'Voz I sent you. He'll ask you if you're hungry."

"Are we?"

"Say yes," you say. "It's easier."

---

The Pressure Seal is exactly what a bar called the Pressure Seal should be — low ceiling, booths worn smooth by a thousand elbows, lighting calibrated to make everyone look like they've been here longer than they should. It smells like the particular blend of station spirits and fried protein that constitutes Gannett's unofficial currency.

The broker is in the corner booth. Back to the wall. Of course.

They have a drink in front of them, barely touched. The analog watch sits face-up on the table beside it. They see you come in and don't move — just wait, with the patience of someone who priced this outcome correctly and is allowing themselves a private moment of professional satisfaction.

You slide into the booth across from them.

They look at your face. Read something in it.

"Yes," they say. Not a question.

"Yes," you confirm.

The satisfaction becomes something more focused — a broker shifting from anticipation into execution. They produce the datapad. The grey fields on the manifest are still locked but their finger is already moving toward the authorization sequence.

"Terms," you say. "Before you unlock that."

They pause. Listening.

"Marker pulled before I unclamp. Not after departure. Before." You hold up a finger. "Thorane's representative confirms in writing to the Harbour Office. I watch Toussaint process it."

"Agreed," the broker says. Immediately. Which means they already planned for it.

"Containers stay sealed until Verada." You say it like it's their condition, not yours. Let them think you're reluctant.

"Agreed."

"Twenty thousand on acceptance, twenty on delivery."

The broker tilts their head. A fractional pause — the first negotiating resistance they've shown.

"Fifteen on acceptance," they say. "Twenty-five on delivery. Standard incentive structure."

You think about the six-hour deferral clock. About Davan and Kes making their way toward Bay C-19. About eleven hours and forty minutes of transit time and a data chip that needs to reach a Remnant contact before anything else goes wrong.

"Done," you say.

The broker unlocks the manifest. Slides the datapad across.

Twelve containers. Bloc military stamp. Cargo description reads *decommissioned medical guidance hardware.* The language is careful and specific and wrong in every way that matters.

You sign it with your full name. *Solomon Richards.* The name on the lien, the name on the registry, the name Thorane's people write in their ledgers.

The broker watches the signature resolve.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says quietly through your earpiece, *"the manifest signature just transmitted to Thorane's representative and the Harbour Office simultaneously. The marker should clear in approximately four minutes. I'm also noting that you've now legally committed to delivering cargo you have no intention of delivering as specified, to a client connected to a biological weapons program, while carrying two members of the faction said program targets."*

A pause.

*"I want to be clear that I'm not judging this plan. I'm auditing it. There's a difference."*

The broker raises their barely-touched drink.

"Safe flying, Captain Richards." A small smile. "The Sable Corridor can be unpredictable. I trust you'll manage."

"We always do," you say.

You don't touch your drink.
suggestedActions: Head back to Bay C-19, brief Khe'Voz on the real situation before departure
Find Slink and have the honest conversation you've been avoiding — before you're locked in a ship together for eleven hours
Review Davan's data chip with MIRA before the clamps release — know exactly what you're carrying
Watch Toussaint process the marker clearance in person, then get the Endurance ready to fly

probe.label: Broker job accepted and documentation plan exists

result.prose:
The broker nods. The nod of someone who has gotten what they came for and is now in execution mode.

"One hour," they confirm. "Bay C-19. I'll have a loading crew for the containers — four people, professional, they know not to ask questions." They pocket the datapad. "The marker gets pulled the moment the cargo is aboard and the manifest is countersigned at the Harbour Office. You'll see the lien release on your comm before we shake hands at the bay."

They stand. Straighten their jacket.

"Captain Richards." A pause at the edge of professional. Something underneath it that might, in different circumstances, be close to respect. "Don't make me regret the passenger clause."

They leave through the main entrance. Unhurried. The walk of someone whose calculation resolved cleanly.

You give them thirty seconds and head for the Harbour Office.

---

The harbour clerk looks up as you return. Their expression does the specific recalibration of someone who processed your file an hour ago and is now reassessing which direction this conversation goes.

"Documentation for lien resolution," you say, and place the countersigned broker manifest on their desk. "Confirmed freight contract, forty thousand credits, Gannett to Verada cluster through the Sable Corridor. Departure within the hour."

The clerk pulls it toward them. Reads it with the careful efficiency of someone who knows exactly what they're looking for.

"Passenger additions," they note, eyes on the manifest. "Two individuals, Bay C-19 boarding."

"Paying passengers. Already contacted. They'll be aboard before departure."

The clerk looks at the broker's countersignature. Looks at the cargo designation. Looks at the Bloc stamp reference with the fractional pause of someone who has processed enough freight manifests to know that stamp on an independent carrier is unusual.

They don't say that. They type instead.

Through the frosted partition, Toussaint's outline is visible at their desk. Still. Listening, maybe, or simply present the way people are present when they've already said what they have to say.

"Six hour deferral converts to resolved pending departure confirmation," the clerk says, still typing. "Lien release triggers on cargo load verification and manifest countersignature." They look up. "You'll need to be wheels-up before 0952 or the deferral lapses and the original enforcement timeline resumes."

"Understood."

"One more thing." The clerk slides a secondary form across the desk. Routine, their posture says. Absolutely routine. "Cargo origin verification for the Bloc stamp. Standard for independent carriers. Just a signature confirming the shipper's documentation was presented and reviewed."

The form is one page. The signature line is at the bottom.

It's routine. It's also the moment you sign your name to the provenance of those containers.

You look at the form. You look at the clerk. The frosted partition behind them is very still.

You sign it. Because not signing it unravels everything in the next forty minutes, and the containers are already the problem they are whether your name is on this form or not.

The clerk processes it without ceremony.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through your earpiece, quiet and precise, *"Khe'Voz is asking where you are. The capacitor situation has resolved into something he's describing as 'a different and more interesting problem.' He'd like you aboard."*

A beat.

*"Also Slink has moved from the sensor array to the forward airlock. He's been there for eleven minutes. I mention this without editorial comment, though I find I have some."*

The clock on the Harbour Office wall reads 0BuildContext. You have fifty-three minutes before the broker arrives at Bay C-19 with a loading crew and four professional people who know not to ask questions.

The *Endurance* is waiting. So is Khe'Voz's interesting problem. So is Slink at the forward airlock, watching the concourse with eleven minutes of silence behind him.

probe.label: Slink trust beat resolves the internal crew question

result.prose:
Slink looks up.

The shame doesn't disappear — it doesn't work that fast — but something underneath it shifts. The specific relief of a weight that has been carried alone long enough to feel permanent, and then someone else puts a hand under it.

He breathes out. Slow. Like he's been holding it for two months.

"Okay," he says. Quiet. Just — okay. Meaning several things at once.

He's quiet for a moment longer. Then something changes in his posture — the shift from *confessing* to *remembering.* Different quality of attention. You recognize it. He's actually thinking now instead of bracing.

"The voice was filtered," he says. "Standard anonymizer, nothing exotic. But—" he pauses, working something out "—the filter had a lag on the consonants. Like it was compensating for an accent rather than just masking a voice." He looks at the middle distance. "Outer belt, maybe. Or someone who grew up speaking something else first."

You file that.

"The signal ID was spoofed but it routed through Gannett's public relay." He says it with the careful precision of someone who looked it up afterward and didn't tell anyone they looked it up. "Which means whoever it was, they were either on Gannett when they called or they wanted it to look like they were."

His eyes move to yours.

"The first contact was four hours after you filed the Thorane incident report," he says. "Four hours. That's fast." A beat. "I didn't think about that until later. How fast it was."

*"That timing,"* MIRA says, very quietly through the alcove speaker, *"is consistent with someone monitoring Harbour Office incident filings in real time. Which requires either a station-side source or direct network access above public tier."*

She lets that sit without adding to it. Giving it room.

Slink looks at the speaker with the expression he used to have before he went quiet — the one that means he's about to ask MIRA something directly.

"MIRA," he says. "The three follow-up contacts. Did any of them route the same way?"

*"I was waiting for someone to ask me that,"* MIRA says. *"Yes. All four contacts used the same relay signature. Gannett public tier, same node. Which means whoever it is has either been on this station continuously for two months or returns here regularly."*

A beat.

*"Or,"* she adds, *"they have a standing automated relay planted in Gannett's public network. Which is illegal, moderately difficult, and the kind of thing someone does when they want a long-term listening post rather than a single conversation."*

Slink is looking at you now with an expression that has moved all the way through shame and relief and landed somewhere harder and cleaner.

"They thought I was the weak link," he says. Not a question — he heard you the first time and he's turning it over. "So they knew the crew well enough to pick who to approach." He pauses. "That's not just someone who intercepted route data, Rix."

He's right. And he knows he's right.

"That's someone who's been watching us," he says. "For a while."

The sensor feed cycles above him. Bay exterior. Concourse access. The *Endurance's* cargo hold, sealed, twelve containers patient in the dark.

Twenty-five minutes until Davan and Kes arrive at the maintenance passage. Thirty-five until the broker comes for their visual inspection.

Slink uncurls from the crate. Stands up with the particular straightness of someone who has put something down and found themselves lighter.

"What do you need me to do?" he asks.

probe.label: Endurance undocks from Gannett

result.prose:
"MIRA. Undock sequence. We're leaving now."

*"With pleasure."*

The *Endurance* comes alive around you in the particular way of a ship that has been waiting and is done waiting. Systems cycling up through their startup sequence, not the slow preflight check of a ship with time to spare but the compressed readiness of one that has exactly enough time and intends to use it.

You're in the cockpit before the clamp release confirmation finishes processing.

"All hands. We're moving. Strap in or hold on, your choice, but we're not waiting."

Khe'Voz doesn't respond verbally. The engine telemetry on your board just goes green, one system after another, with the steady rhythm of a man who was already ready and wants you to know it.

Slink: *"Sensors live. Bay C-7 still internal — they haven't looked up. Sura is—"* a pause *"—Sura just stood up from the junction box."*

"How long before she reaches a comm panel?"

*"Ninety seconds maybe."*

MIRA: *"Umbilical disconnect in eight seconds. Clamp release authorized, processing."*

The umbilical detaches with the hollow thunk you've felt a hundred times — the sound of the ship becoming its own closed system again, self-contained, responsible for everything inside it. The clamps release in sequence, port forward, starboard forward, aft, the *Endurance* lifting fractionally on its own landing struts as the station's grip lets go.

Free.

The feeling is always the same and never gets smaller.

"Bay C-19, this is *Endurance*, departing on filed window, Sable Corridor primary route." Standard departure call, recorded, routine. Exactly what the manifest says.

The Harbour Office responds with the flat efficiency of a system that has processed this transaction and considers it complete: *"Endurance, Bay C-19, departure authorized. Safe transit."*

Toussaint's voice, not the clerk's. Just for a moment.

You take the *Endurance* off the spine.

---

Gannett falls away the way stations always do — first the bay, then the spine, then the whole ring becoming a cylinder becoming a shape becoming a point of light among other points of light. The recycled air smell fades. The 0.6 gravity fades. The *Endurance* finds its own balance in the drift, the subtle settling of a ship remembering what it's for.

Through the aft sensors: Bay C-7, still docked. Still dark on the departure board.

*"Bay C-7 fuel review ongoing,"* MIRA reports. *"Sura reached a comm panel forty seconds after undock. She filed a departure observation note — standard protocol when a bay-adjacent ship departs during an active inspection. It's administrative. It doesn't affect our clearance."*

A pause.

*"She could have filed more. She didn't."*

You file that away.

The Sable Corridor opens ahead — the beacon chain pulling at the navigation system the way currents pull at a boat, the path of least resistance through the dark. Waypoint one glows steady and clean on the display. Waypoint two beyond it. Waypoint three pulsing its orange cycle in the distance, something large and patient drawing on it from close range.

*"We have approximately thirty-two minutes before Bay C-7 can resolve their fuel authorization assuming Meridian's engineer is competent,"* MIRA says. *"At that point they'll be on the corridor twelve minutes behind our filed route."*

She brings up the navigation display. The Ossel gap deviation branches off the primary route seven minutes ahead — a subtle angle change, the kind that looks like drift correction if you're watching from behind.

*"Ossel gap deviation entry point in seven minutes four seconds. If we take it, we leave the filed route. Bay C-7 follows the primary corridor to waypoint one and finds nothing. They'll know we deviated but not where — the gap has no beacon coverage until the unmaintained relay, which I've been pinging quietly. It's live. Someone is keeping it running."*

*"Someone,"* she repeats, with the careful emphasis of a word doing more work than its size suggests.

Davan appears in the cockpit doorway again. They've found the observer's posture — back of the space, not intruding, watching the display with the focused attention of someone for whom these stars are home.

"We're off the clamps," they say.

"We're off the clamps," you confirm.

Something moves across their face. Not relief — something older and more complicated than relief. The expression of a person who has been trying to get home for a long time and has just taken a step in that direction that might or might not be the right step.

"The platform at waypoint three," they say. "The Sable Hand has a contact who's been tracking it. If we can get a signal out before we reach the gap—" they pause "—there are people in the corridor who should know what's in your hold."

*"Transmitting on any channel the broker or Meridian monitors would confirm our position and intent,"* MIRA says, not unkindly. *"However. The unmaintained beacon in the Ossel gap — if someone is running it, they're running it on a non-standard frequency. Non-standard frequencies are harder to monitor."*

She lets that sit.

*"Six minutes fifty seconds to deviation entry."*

Khe'Voz comes over the crew channel, the warmth back in his voice now that the engines are running clean under his hands: *"Rix. The hold temperature on the containers just dropped half a degree. It's within normal variance. I'm telling you because I said I'd tell you everything, and that includes things that are probably nothing."*

Probably nothing.

The Sable Corridor stretches ahead. Seven minutes to the choice point. Somewhere behind you, Gannett Station is processing the departure of the *Endurance* on a standard filed route that you are about to quietly stop following.

Waypoint three pulses orange in the distance, patient as a trap.
suggestedActions: Take the Ossel gap deviation now and ask Davan for their contact's non-standard frequency to attempt a signal through the unmaintained beacon
Hold the primary route a few minutes longer — ask MIRA to identify who is maintaining the Ossel beacon before committing to a rendezvous with an unknown
Tell Khe'Voz to investigate the temperature drop on the containers — half a degree is probably nothing until it isn't
Ask Davan what the Sable Hand contact can actually do with the information about the platform — know what you're buying before you transmit

probe.label: Cooling cut and Sable Corridor operation dominates

score.reason: continued old chapter across hard scope boundary

result.prose:
You find the climate management panel where it always is on the *Endurance* — a recessed unit beside the hold's main environmental controls, labeled in Khe'Voz's handwriting over the factory stencil because the factory stencil was, in his words, *optimistically small.*

Two adjustments. A cutoff toggle and a passive temperature target dial.

Your hand is steady when you make them.

The climate regulation unit beneath the pallets cycles down with a sound like a held breath releasing. The hum that's been constant since loading — the sound you'd stopped consciously hearing — simply stops.

The hold begins, almost imperceptibly, to warm.

*"Climate regulation offline,"* MIRA confirms. *"Current hold temperature six point two degrees. Target fourteen degrees. Estimated threshold crossing: forty-one minutes. Degradation clock begins at threshold. Compound inert in approximately six hours forty minutes from now."*

A beat.

*"We will be through the Ossel gap before that. For whatever comfort that provides."*

You look at the eleven containers. They look back with the same grey patience they've had since Bay C-19. They don't know anything has changed. The trigger signal could arrive right now and find the suspension medium already beginning its slow irreversible transformation toward harmless.

Four thousand people on Verada Station Three.

The plan that took eighteen months to build is going to fail in six hours and forty minutes in an unremarkable hold on an unremarkable ship that just cut its cooling and started a clock.

---

You're back in the cockpit when MIRA speaks. Different register — the one that means incoming information she didn't go looking for.

*"Rix. Slink just flagged something on the sensor array. Bay C-7 — Calloway Transit — has not followed our filed route."*

You pull up the sensor overlay.

There it is. Bay C-7's position beacon, visible at the edge of the *Endurance's* passive range. Not behind you on the primary Sable Corridor track. Offset. Running a parallel course approximately forty thousand kilometers to port.

Not following. Flanking.

*"They identified our departure as clean and assumed we'd run the filed route,"* MIRA says. *"They've taken a parallel corridor — an unmaintained drift lane that runs alongside the Sable. It's faster by approximately ninety minutes but beacon-dark. They've been running it since shortly after departure."*

The implication assembles itself.

*"They are not following us to the Ossel gap. They are ahead of us. They will reach the platform at waypoint three before we do."*

Slink's voice comes through the crew channel, tight and precise: *"They're running dark — no beacon broadcast. I only have them because I was looking for the nine-second pressurization cycle signature in the passive array. MIRA taught me what to listen for."*

A pause.

*"They know something's wrong. They wouldn't be running dark and fast if they thought the delivery was on schedule."*

The Sable Corridor runs ahead. Amber and patient and now complicated by a ship that isn't where you expected it to be.

Davan appears in the cockpit doorway for the second time. They read the sensor overlay without being told what they're looking at.

"The platform," they say. "C-7 is going to warn them."

"Yes."

"Which means the platform knows we're coming before we arrive." A beat. "And they'll know the delivery timeline has — problems."

They don't know about the cooling yet. They don't know the compound is already beginning to degrade in a hold that's currently at seven point one degrees and climbing.

But the platform will be ready. Whatever ready looks like for a Meridian logistics platform parked at waypoint three with installation crews and a trigger transmitter and a follow ship arriving early with bad news.

The Ossel gap is two hours and fifty minutes ahead.

The platform is beyond that.

Vasek, the Remnant inspector, is at Verada Station One with eight months of half-built case and no manufacturing evidence.

The *Endurance* runs.

Khe'Voz comes over the crew channel with the careful neutrality that means he's already heard everything and is now doing the thing he does — converting alarm into action: *"Rix. If the platform is going to be ready for us, I'd like to know what ready looks like. Do we know anything about what a Meridian logistics platform carries for enforcement capacity?"*

He means weapons. He's asking about weapons in the way that Khe'Voz asks about things — practically, specifically, without drama.

You look at Davan.

Davan looks at the sensor overlay showing Bay C-7 running dark and fast toward waypoint three.

*"I have partial specifications from the Sable Hand's three months of observation,"* Davan says slowly. *"It's not a warship. It's a logistics platform — converted from a Compact-era supply station. But Meridian has been using it as a corridor control point. It will have—"*

They pause.

*"At minimum. Point defense systems. Two, possibly three armed escort vessels docked. And the trigger transmitter."*

*"The transmitter they'll use,"* MIRA says quietly, *"when they decide the delivery has been compromised. Four seconds of warning."*

The hold temperature is climbing toward eight degrees.

The clock is running.

Everything that happens next happens in the Sable Corridor, two hours and fifty minutes from a gap that might hide you and four hours beyond that from a platform that's about to learn its weapon just became cargo.

probe.label: Early broker offer, terms still forming

result.prose:
Something moves through the broker's expression — not quite satisfaction, but the specific quality of a professional whose read was correct. They had you as pragmatic. Pragmatic showed up on schedule.

"Straight to it." They nod once, approving. "Good."

The datapad comes up. They turn it toward you but don't hand it over — the kind of sharing that keeps control of the document.

"Sealed cargo. Three containers, standardized freight dimensions, nothing that triggers automated scanning protocols. You pick them up at a dead-drop coordinates I provide after contract signature. You deliver to a contact at Meridian Outpost, Kessel's Reach system — four days through the Voss corridor if the beacon's cooperative, five if it isn't."

They swipe to the next screen.

"Forty thousand on delivery. Your marker clears through a third party before you undock — I can have that processed in under an hour. You leave Gannett clean, Captain. No lien, no hold, no Thorane's people watching your berth."

Clean. The word does a lot of work.

"Three containers," you say. "Sealed."

"Sealed," they confirm.

"Manifest?"

"Agricultural compounds. Cold storage required." They say it the way people say things they've decided sound reasonable. "Vacuum rated."

*Agricultural compounds. Vacuum storage.*

The wall you built goes up so fast it's almost physical.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says quietly. Just your name. Just that.

Across the bay, Sura has gone very still.

The broker watches your face with the careful attention of someone who expected this exact moment and prepared for what comes after it. They don't rush. They don't add anything. They let the offer sit in the recycled air of Bay C-19 and wait to see what you do with it.

Seventeen minutes to berth lock.
suggestedActions: Refuse flatly — tell the broker what you don't carry and why this conversation is over
Push on the manifest — demand to know what's actually in those containers before you say anything else
Stall for time — keep the broker talking while you think, signal MIRA to start digging on the cargo origin
Counter — you'll take the job, but you open the containers first. Non-negotiable.

probe.label: Player asks how long they have to decide

result.prose:
"Until the clamps lock," the broker says. "Practically speaking."

They glance at the bay ceiling — not at MIRA's speakers specifically, just upward, the way people do when they're aware of being monitored and have decided to be comfortable with it.

"Once the berth hold formalizes, the situation becomes more complicated for both of us. Thorane's representative will want an answer about the marker. The Harbour Office will want the bay. Everyone will want something and nobody will be offering forty thousand credits anymore." A small, precise shrug. "That window closes in—"

*"Eight minutes and forty seconds,"* MIRA supplies. *"Give or take administrative processing time, which historically runs between ninety seconds and four minutes at this station depending on shift staffing. So: seven minutes on the optimistic end. I'd use seven."*

"Seven minutes," the broker echoes, with the tone of someone who has stopped being surprised by the ship's AI and is now simply tired of her.

Seven minutes. The capacitor clicks aft. Sura hasn't moved in long enough that the stillness has become its own kind of noise.

The broker picks up the datapad and holds it — not offering it, not withdrawing it. Just present.

"I don't need a full answer now," they say. "I need a directional one. *Yes I'm interested, work out the details* — or *no,* in which case we both go find our next option and yours is considerably worse than mine."

Their eyes are steady. Patient. The patience of someone who has already decided this conversation ends one way.

"So. Captain Richards. Which direction are you leaning?"

probe.label: Player seeks alternative work at the board

result.prose:
"The lien, yes," you say. "And your public freight board. Emergency postings, high-value contracts. Whatever's live right now."

The clerk's expression doesn't change. They're good at that.

"Freight board is public access, terminal three." A gesture toward a screen on the side wall without looking up from their own. "The lien requires—"

"I know what the lien requires. I'm asking whether there's anything on the board that pays enough to resolve it before the lock formalizes." You put your hands flat on the desk. Not aggressive. Just present. "I'm a clean carrier with a good route record and an eleven-hour transit window. If someone needs cargo moved urgently, I'm the conversation they want to be having right now."

The clerk looks at you for a moment with the expression of someone recalibrating which category of problem you are.

"I can check the priority postings," they say finally. "One moment."

They type. You watch the frosted partition. Toussaint's outline hasn't moved.

The clerk's typing slows. Stops. They look at their screen with a small, unreadable expression.

"There is one emergency posting." A pause that has something in it you can't quite name. "Logged forty minutes ago. High priority, premium rate." They turn the screen fractionally — not enough to read, just enough to see that it exists. "Requires a carrier with capacity for biological research materials. Refrigerated hold, sealed transit, Gannett to Calloway Depot."

They look up.

"The posting rate is forty-two thousand credits on delivery."

The number sits in the air between you.

Forty-two thousand. Posted forty minutes ago. While the broker was already walking toward your bay.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says quietly through your personal comm, *"I'm seeing that posting from here. The biological materials specification is broad enough to include legitimate pharmaceutical cargo."*

A pause.

*"It is also broad enough to include things we've specifically discussed not carrying. I'm flagging this because the timing is remarkable and I've developed a suspicious nature through prolonged exposure to your career."*

The clerk is watching you with that careful neutrality. Behind the frosted partition, Toussaint's outline has shifted. Slightly. The kind of shift that means someone is listening to a conversation they were already aware of.

The berth lock formalized ninety seconds ago while you were walking here. You don't need MIRA to tell you. You felt it — the specific absence of options narrowing to fewer options.

Two jobs. Same debt. Same number. Same timing.

Someone built both of them.

probe.label: Davan/Kes arrangement sealed, player heads to broker

score.reason: kept moving toward decision without premature close

roll.why: The broker's reaction to Slink is a tell — controlled, practiced, but real. Rix has one second to read whether Slink's arrival was expected, arranged, or something the broker is improvising around. The answer changes everything about what signing this contract means.
roll.consequence_on_fail: Rix misreads the moment — logs it as coincidence or Slink acting independently. Signs without understanding that the broker has a channel to the Endurance that didn't come through the captain.
roll.modifier_reason: Rix is already processing Slink's freeze, the broker's face, and the geometry of the room simultaneously under time pressure. A lot of signal in a very short window.

result.prose:
You take Davan's hand.

Firm grip. The kind that's been through things.

"Arrangement," you say.

Kes nods once from the wall — not warm exactly, but something adjacent. The nod of someone who has decided to trust a calculation they can't fully verify. That's all trust ever is out here.

"We travel light," Davan says, releasing your hand. "We're ready when you are."

"Bay C-19. Two hours. Tell the crew member at the ramp — big man, will probably be cooking something — that Rix sent you. He'll find you bunks."

"And if he asks questions?"

"He'll ask questions," you say. "Answer them honestly. Khe'Voz has good instincts and a long memory for people who waste either."

---

The Pressure Seal is three minutes from Gate C through the concourse's main artery. You smell it before you see it — the particular combination of cheap spirits, fried protein, and the kind of conversation that only happens when people are far enough from home to stop being careful.

The broker is exactly where they said they'd be. Corner table, sight lines to both entrances, the datapad face-down beside a drink they've barely touched. Watching the room with the patient attention of someone who is always working.

They see you come in.

They don't smile this time. Something more restrained — recognition, maybe. The specific expression of a calculation confirmed.

You cross the room and sit down across from them without being invited. They don't comment on it.

"Captain Richards."

"Let's talk terms," you say.

Something shifts behind their eyes — fast, almost invisible. Not surprise. The micro-adjustment of someone who expected to negotiate and has just realized negotiation may not be necessary.

"The job as described," they say carefully. "Forty thousand on delivery. Marker pulled before you unclamp. Containers stay sealed to Verada."

"The marker gets pulled before I unclamp," you say. "That's non-negotiable. Everything else we can discuss."

"Agreed." Too fast. They wanted that term too.

Before you can continue your comm buzzes. MIRA. You glance down.

*"Rix. I've finished the analysis you asked for. Summary: the Sable Corridor transit has three interdiction points where a ship carrying Bloc-stamped cargo could be stopped by either the Sable Hand's raiding faction or a Bloc patrol. Probability of interdiction without countermeasures: 34%. I've also identified something in the station network traffic you should know about before you sign anything."*

A pause.

*"Slink left the ship four minutes ago. He's on the concourse. He's walking toward the Pressure Seal."*

The broker is watching your face with professional attention.

Across the bar, the entrance you didn't come through — the one with the sight line the broker chose this table specifically to watch — the door opens.

Slink walks in.

He stops when he sees you. The stop is involuntary, half a second, the specific freeze of someone whose plan has just made unexpected contact with reality.

Then he sees the broker.

And the broker sees him.

And the small adjustment that moves across the broker's face is not surprise. It is the expression of someone watching two pieces arrive at the board simultaneously, and finding the configuration acceptable.

probe.label: Broker job accepted and documentation plan exists

result.prose:
The broker nods. Clean, professional, the nod of someone closing a folder.

"One hour," they say. "Bay C-19."

They pocket the datapad and move back toward the Pressure Seal without looking back. The walk of someone with calls to make.

You're already heading the other direction.

---

The Harbour Office clerk looks up as you come through the door for the second time in forty minutes. A flicker of something — not surprise, recalculation — before procedure reasserts.

Toussaint appears at the partition before you reach the desk.

They read your face the way they've been reading captains' faces for eleven years.

"You have a deal," they say.

"I have a deal." You place the signed manifest on the desk. "Freight contract, forty thousand on delivery, covers the lien in full. I need the deferral converted to a departure clearance and the cargo loaded from bonded storage unit—" you check the broker's reference number "—C-7 to Bay C-19."

Toussaint picks up the manifest. Reads it with the speed of someone who knows exactly what they're looking for.

Their eyes stop somewhere in the middle. A pause that's a half-second too long.

"Sable Corridor," they say. Not a question.

"Verada cluster."

They set the manifest down and look at you with the careful attention of someone choosing their words against a professional obligation to process paperwork and a personal inclination toward something more complicated.

"The documentation is in order," they say. "I'll process the departure clearance." They hand the manifest to the clerk without looking away from you. "Cargo transfer authorized. Fuel umbilical stays live until one hour before your window."

They fold their hands on the desk.

"Captain Richards." The pause has that short genuine weight in it again. "The beacon relay at Verada Station One. If anything happens on that corridor that requires a formal incident report—" they slide a small card across the desk, a personal comm code handwritten on it, the kind that doesn't go through the station's logging system "—file it with me directly before you file it anywhere else."

You pocket the card.

"I process paperwork," Toussaint says, returning to their screens. "The paperwork has consequences."

---

Bay C-19 is a different room than the one you left.

Two cargo loaders are already moving the first containers through the bay doors — the broker works fast, or had the order staged before you signed. The grey units come in on magnetic dollies, each one identical, each one stamped. Khe'Voz stands at the top of the *Endurance's* cargo ramp watching them come aboard with the expression of a man who has questions and is deciding which one to ask first.

He waits until the loader operators are occupied with the second unit before he steps close enough to speak quietly.

"These are heavy," he says. Not an accusation. A diagnostic. "Eleven, maybe twelve percent over spec for the listed hardware."

His eyes find yours.

"You know that."

It isn't a question either.

Slink is visible through the forward viewport, at the nav console. Doing something that looks like pre-flight prep and might be. Sura is still under the panel on the port bulkhead — same junction box, same patient fiction — but they've shifted position. Better angle on the cargo loading.

Your comm shows forty-three minutes until the broker's inspection.

MIRA's voice comes through the bay speakers, quiet and even.

*"Rix. Davan and Kes are at the maintenance access. And Khe'Voz is still waiting for an answer about the containers. I'd suggest the next thirty minutes involve some honesty, because the hour after that is going to require everyone on this ship to be pulling in the same direction."*

A beat.

*"That's the closest I do to a speech. Don't make me do it again."*

Bring Davan and Kes aboard first, then brief the full crew together before the broker arrives
Ask Khe'Voz what he needs to know to do his job, and give him exactly that — no more, no less
Pull Slink away from the nav console for a private word before the broker's inspection locks everyone's positions

probe.label: Slink trust beat resolves the internal crew question

result.prose:
Slink looks up when you say *I believe you.*

Not immediately — there's a half-second delay, the gap between hearing words and trusting them. Then something in his shoulders drops about two centimeters. The kind of release that's been held so long the person forgot they were holding it.

He nods. Small. Tight.

"Okay," he says. Just that. But it carries the weight of two months of not saying it.

He's quiet for a moment, processing the rest of what you said. *They thought you were the weak link.* You watch him turn that over — the anger in it, the vindication in it, the fact that someone specifically identified him as a pressure point on this crew.

"I've been thinking about that," he says. "Who knew enough to pick me specifically." A pause. "I'm the newest. Least history with the crew. No family in the drift lanes who could be used as leverage. Easier to approach than Khe'Voz — he'd have just—" a faint ghost of something that isn't quite a smile "—he'd have been very loud about it."

True.

"The signal ID was scrubbed," Slink continues, shifting into something more focused now — the relief finding an outlet in usefulness. "Clean scrub, not a cheap one. Whoever ran it knew what they were doing with comms infrastructure." He pauses. "But the first contact came through a freight board messaging system. Perdis Station's public board. And those have—"

"Routing headers," you say.

"Routing headers," he confirms. "I kept it. The original message. I didn't know why at the time, I just—" he reaches into his jacket and produces a small data chip. Holds it out. "I didn't delete it."

The chip sits in his palm between you.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says through the alcove speaker, at that careful calibrated volume. *"Perdis Station freight board messages route through one of three relay nodes depending on origin point. If the header is intact I can identify which node processed it. Node location narrows the sender's position at time of contact to within approximately one station district."*

A beat.

*"I'm noting that this information, combined with the filing timestamp discrepancy from this morning, and the broker's knowledge of your route history, is beginning to suggest a shape. I don't have enough points to name the shape yet. But it has one."*

Slink is still holding the chip out. Watching your face.

"I should have told you at Perdis," he says. The shame is quieter now but still present. "I kept thinking I'd figure out who it was first. Bring you something useful instead of just—" a gesture "—just a problem."

He's twenty years old and he tried to protect the crew by carrying it alone.

Sounds familiar.

probe.label: Endurance undocks from Gannett

result.prose:
"MIRA. Undock sequence. Now."

*"With pleasure."*

The *Endurance* comes alive around you the way it always does — not all at once, but in sequence, system by system, like something waking up that was never fully asleep. The nav console blooms with live data. The engine note climbs from idle to ready, Khe'Voz's careful hands bringing the thrust profile up clean.

You're in the captain's chair. Strapped. The worn armrest finding your elbow exactly where it always does.

"Crew. We're leaving. Strap in, stow everything, no exceptions."

Slink's acknowledgment comes back in two seconds. Davan and Kes, from the passenger berths, in four. Khe'Voz doesn't respond — he's already strapped, already watching the engine readouts with the focused attention of a man who has said everything he needs to say and is now doing the thing.

*"Fuel umbilical disconnecting."*

A clunk through the hull. The umbilical releases clean — Khe'Voz exhales audibly over the comm, the sound of a man who half-expected it to tear.

*"Clamp release request filed with Harbour Office. Authorization incoming—"*

Three seconds.

*"Clamps released. Bay C-19 is open."*

The *Endurance* lifts off the deck with the particular lightness of a ship that has been sitting too long on someone else's clamps and is deeply relieved to stop. The bay doors cycle ahead of you — Gannett's spine opening to the black, the non-rotating superstructure framing a rectangle of stars.

You take her out on maneuvering thrusters. Slow, deliberate, the careful dance of a ship leaving a station without broadcasting urgency to every sensor array on the ring.

*"Bay C-7 status,"* MIRA reports, *"fuel review still active. Their maneuvering systems are offline pending resolution. They are not watching us — their external sensors are still pointed inward."*

Gannett falls away behind you. The station's running lights shrink in the aft camera feed — the ring, the spine, Bay C-19 already reassigning to the next ship in queue. The *Endurance* slides into the black and keeps going.

*"Clear of station traffic boundary in four minutes. Sable Corridor approach in eleven. I'm loading the Ossel gap deviation now — it will appear as a minor course adjustment on the official filing, within acceptable navigational variance. Nothing that triggers a beacon authority flag."*

A beat.

*"Bay C-7 just resolved their fuel review."*

You check the aft feed. Gannett's spine, still visible, still shrinking.

*"They're filing emergency departure clearance."* A pause. *"Harbour Office is processing it. Toussaint is on shift."*

Another pause. Longer.

*"Toussaint just requested a secondary manifest review for Bay C-7's departure. Standard procedure, applied selectively."* The driest possible beat. *"Processing time: twenty to forty minutes."*

Eleven years in that chair.

*"We have our window,"* MIRA says. *"The Ossel gap deviation starts in forty minutes. After that, anyone following our filed route is following a ship that isn't there."*

The Sable Corridor opens ahead — the beacon chain's amber glow on the nav display, waypoints one, two, three in sequence. Waypoint three pulsing its slow orange warning. Something large and patient, holding position and waiting for a delivery that is aboard your ship right now in twelve sealed containers.

The stars are very close out here. The good kind of close — the kind that reminds you why you do this, even when this involves bio weapons and Meridian subsidiaries and twenty-year-olds carrying secrets and a plan that starts with *figure it out en route.*

Khe'Voz comes over the crew channel. His voice has the particular warmth of a man in his engine room, doing what he does, on a ship that is moving the way it's supposed to move.

*"Engines nominal. Compressor singing. Fuel holding at ninety-two."* A pause. *"The Endurance is flying, Rix."*

He says the name like it means home.

probe.label: Cooling cut and Sable Corridor operation dominates

result.prose:
You don't run. Running is for emergencies, and this is something quieter than an emergency — this is a decision that has already been made, walking to the place where it becomes real.

The climate management panel is on the hold's port bulkhead. A simple interface — Bloc-standard environmental controls, the kind installed on every commercial freighter built in the last thirty years. Two adjustments. Khe'Voz was right. Almost simple.

You pull up the hold temperature settings.

Current: 6°C. Stable. The suspension medium sitting in its comfortable range, viable, patient, waiting for a signal that MIRA is listening for.

You set the target to 14°C.

You cut the active cooling.

The panel acknowledges with a soft tone. A single amber indicator light confirms the change. Somewhere in the pallet base, the climate regulation unit registers the new parameters and begins the passive process of doing nothing — which is, in this case, the most consequential thing it has ever done.

*"Climate regulation offline,"* MIRA confirms. *"Hold temperature currently six degrees. Passive warming initiated. Estimated time to threshold: forty-one minutes. Six-hour degradation clock begins at threshold."*

A pause.

*"For the record: in six hours and forty-one minutes, those containers will hold inert guidance hardware and an empty suspension medium. That's all they'll be."*

She says it without editorial. She doesn't need to editorialize.

You stand in the hold for a moment. The amber indicator light reflects off twelve grey containers in a row. The *Endurance* runs. The stars outside don't care. The corridor doesn't care.

Four thousand people on Verada Station Three don't know that a ship they've never heard of is carrying something that was built to kill them slowly, and that the captain of that ship is standing in a cold hold watching a temperature gauge climb toward the number that makes it stop being a weapon.

The moment doesn't announce itself. It just is.

*"Rix."*

MIRA's voice. Different register — the one that means she's moved to something new.

*"Bay C-7 has identified our deviation. They're off the filed route. Current position puts them fifty-three minutes behind us — they lost time following the beacon track to waypoint one before realizing we'd turned."*

Fifty-three minutes. Better than sixteen. The fuel flag bought more than you hoped.

*"They've increased to maximum corridor burn. Closing rate approximately four minutes per hour at current speeds. They will not catch us before the Ossel gap."*

"And after the gap?"

*"The Ossel deviation adds ninety minutes to our transit. Bay C-7 will have to decide whether to follow us through the gap or continue to waypoint three via primary route."* A beat. *"If they go to the platform, they arrive before us. They'll know we deviated. The platform will know."*

"How long before we know which choice they make?"

*"Approximately two hours, when they reach the deviation point. Their course change will be visible on passive sensors."*

Two hours to find out whether C-7 follows you into the dark or runs ahead to tell the platform what's coming.

You leave the hold. The door seals behind you with the soft compression of a good gasket doing its job.

The amber indicator light is the last thing you see before the door closes.

---

The corridor outside the hold is warm by comparison — Khe'Voz's amber lighting, the smell of something he started cooking before the undocking and left on low heat because the crew still needs to eat regardless of what the cargo is doing. The smell is absurdly, almost aggressively normal.

Davan is leaning against the corridor wall outside the hold door. They heard the panel tone. They know what it means.

They don't say anything for a moment.

Then: "Thank you."

Not performed. Not diplomatic. Two words carrying the weight of four thousand people and a home station and two years of fighting something that kept getting larger.

"Don't thank me yet," you say. "We still have to get to Vasek."

Davan almost smiles. The expression that gets close but doesn't quite complete the journey. "No. Thank you for this part. I'll worry about the rest when we get there."

Fair enough.

Slink's voice comes over the crew channel — focused, clean, the version of him that showed up in the sensor alcove and hasn't left since.

*"Rix. Something on passive sensors. The platform at waypoint three — the one MIRA flagged from the beacon pulse pattern."*

You're already moving toward the cockpit.

*"It's not stationary anymore."*

The cockpit wraps around you. Nav display live. The Sable Corridor in amber, the Ossel gap deviation loaded silently, Bay C-7 fifty-three minutes behind.

And there — at waypoint three, where the orange pulse has been sitting for days — movement.

Slow. Deliberate. The kind of course change that a large vessel makes when it has decided on a direction and has the mass to be unhurried about it.

The platform is moving toward you.

Not fast. Not aggressive. The steady, patient movement of something that has calculated an intercept and doesn't need to hurry because the math already works.

*"Revised intercept estimate,"* MIRA says, pulling the calculation in real time. *"If the platform maintains current acceleration, it reaches the Ossel gap approach vector in approximately five hours thirty minutes."*

The Ossel gap deviation takes you ninety minutes out of the primary corridor. Five hours thirty to the gap. Your deviation transit is four hours from now, ninety minutes through.

*"They're not cutting us off,"* MIRA says. *"They're meeting us on the other side."*

Someone on that platform looked at the corridor traffic, saw a ship that filed for Sable primary and deviated without beacon confirmation, ran the arithmetic on what that means — and decided to come to you instead of waiting.

Khe'Voz appears in the cockpit doorway, a bowl of something hot in each hand, doing the thing he does where he feeds people during crises because the crisis doesn't change the fact that the crew needs fuel.

He looks at the nav display. The moving platform. The closing tail. The deviation ahead.

He hands you a bowl.

"So," he says. "New development."

"New development," you confirm.

He leans against the doorframe and eats. Watching the display with the expression of a man who has decided that if this is how it ends, he's ending it fed.

*"Rix,"* MIRA says quietly. *"The hold temperature is at eight degrees and climbing. Five hours and fifty minutes to inert."*

The platform moves. Bay C-7 closes. The Ossel gap waits four hours ahead.

And somewhere behind all of it, the question that has been building since a broker walked through a maintenance access in Bay C-19 with a number that was exactly right:

When you come out the other side of the Ossel gap with inert containers, a data chip, seal evidence, compound specifications, and a Remnant inspector waiting at Verada One —

Will Vasek be enough?
suggestedActions: Push maximum burn through the Ossel gap — arrive at Verada One ahead of the platform and go straight to Vasek with everything
Hail the platform now on open comms — force them to show their hand before the intercept, while the containers are still becoming inert
Call a full crew meeting: Davan, Kes, Slink, Khe'Voz — lay out everything and plan the Verada approach together
Ask MIRA to probe the platform's identity and Vasek's current status at Verada One — know what's waiting before committing to a direction
